Following Hungary's defeat against the Ottoman Empire in the [Battle of Mohács](/wiki/Battle_of_Moh%C3%A1cs "Battle of Mohács") of 1526, the Habsburg Empire became more involved in the Kingdom of Hungary, and subsequently assumed the Hungarian throne. However, as the Ottomans expanded further into Hungary, the Habsburgs came to control only a small north-western portion of the former kingdom's territory. Eventually, following the [Treaty of Passarowitz](/wiki/Treaty_of_Passarowitz "Treaty of Passarowitz") in 1718, all former territories of the Hungarian kingdom were ceded from the Ottomans to the Habsburgs. In the [revolutions of 1848](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848_in_the_Austrian_Empire "Revolutions of 1848 in the Austrian Empire"), the Kingdom of Hungary called for greater self-government and later even independence from the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"). The ensuing [Hungarian Revolution of 1848](/wiki/Hungarian_Revolution_of_1848 "Hungarian Revolution of 1848") was crushed by the Austrian military with [Russian](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") military assistance, and the level of autonomy that the Hungarian state had enjoyed was replaced with absolutist rule from Vienna.[[22]](#cite_note-:0-30) This further increased Hungarian resentment of the Habsburg dominion.[[23]](#cite_note-31)

In the 1860s, the Empire faced two severe defeats: its loss in the [Second Italian War of Independence](/wiki/Second_Italian_War_of_Independence "Second Italian War of Independence") broke its dominion over a large part of Northern Italy ([Lombardy, Veneto](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Lombardy%E2%80%93Venetia "Kingdom of Lombardy–Venetia"), [Modena, Reggio](/wiki/Duchy_of_Modena_and_Reggio "Duchy of Modena and Reggio"), [Tuscany](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Tuscany "Grand Duchy of Tuscany"), [Parma and Piacenza](/wiki/Duchy_of_Parma_and_Piacenza "Duchy of Parma and Piacenza")) while defeat in the [Austro-Prussian War](/wiki/Austro-Prussian_War "Austro-Prussian War") of 1866 led to the dissolution of the [German Confederation](/wiki/German_Confederation "German Confederation") (of which the Habsburg emperor was the hereditary president) and the exclusion of Austria from German affairs.[[24]](#cite_note-32) These twin defeats gave the Hungarians the opportunity to remove the shackles of absolutist rule.[[25]](#cite_note-33)

Realizing the need to compromise with Hungary in order to retain its great power status, the central government in Vienna began negotiations with the Hungarian political leaders, led by [Ferenc Deák](/wiki/Ferenc_De%C3%A1k_(politician) "Ferenc Deák (politician)"). The Hungarians maintained that the [April Laws](/wiki/April_Laws "April Laws") were still valid, but conceded that under the [Pragmatic Sanction of 1713](/wiki/Pragmatic_Sanction_of_1713 "Pragmatic Sanction of 1713"), foreign affairs and defence were "common" to Austria and Hungary. On 20 March 1867, the newly [re-established Hungarian parliament](/wiki/Diet_of_Hungary "Diet of Hungary") at [Pest](/wiki/Pest,_Hungary "Pest, Hungary") started to negotiate the new laws to be accepted on 30 March. However, Hungarian leaders received word that the Emperor's formal coronation as King of Hungary on 8 June had to have taken place in order for the laws to be enacted within the lands of the [Holy Crown of Hungary](/wiki/Holy_Crown_of_Hungary "Holy Crown of Hungary").[[26]](#cite_note-Kann_1974-34) On 28 July, Franz Joseph, in his new capacity as King of Hungary, approved and promulgated the new laws, which officially gave birth to the Dual Monarchy.

### 1866–1878: Beyond Lesser Germany

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Ferenc_J%C3%B3zsef_koron%C3%A1z%C3%A1sa_Bud%C3%A1n.jpg/250px-Ferenc_J%C3%B3zsef_koron%C3%A1z%C3%A1sa_Bud%C3%A1n.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ferenc_J%C3%B3zsef_koron%C3%A1z%C3%A1sa_Bud%C3%A1n.jpg)

[Coronation](/wiki/Coronation_of_the_Hungarian_monarch "Coronation of the Hungarian monarch") of [Franz Joseph I](/wiki/Franz_Joseph_I_of_Austria "Franz Joseph I of Austria") and [Elisabeth Amalie](/wiki/Empress_Elisabeth_of_Austria "Empress Elisabeth of Austria") at [Matthias Church](/wiki/Matthias_Church "Matthias Church"), [Buda](/wiki/Buda "Buda"), 8 June 1867

The [Austro-Prussian War](/wiki/Austro-Prussian_War "Austro-Prussian War") was ended by the [Peace of Prague (1866)](/wiki/Peace_of_Prague_(1866) "Peace of Prague (1866)") which settled the "[German question](/wiki/German_question "German question")" in favor of a [Lesser German Solution](/wiki/Lesser_Germany "Lesser Germany").[[27]](#cite_note-35) [Count Friedrich Ferdinand von Beust](/wiki/Friedrich_Ferdinand_von_Beust "Friedrich Ferdinand von Beust"), who was the foreign minister from 1866 to 1871, hated the Prussian chancellor, [Otto von Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck"), who had repeatedly outmaneuvered him. Beust looked to France for avenging Austria's defeat and attempted to negotiate with Emperor [Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III") of France and Italy for an anti-Prussian alliance, but no terms could be reached. The decisive victory of the Prusso-German armies in the [Franco-Prussian war](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War") and the subsequent founding of the German Empire ended all hope of re-establishing Austrian influence in Germany, and Beust retired.[[28]](#cite_note-36)

After being forced out of Germany and Italy, the Dual Monarchy turned to the Balkans, which were in tumult as nationalistic movements were gaining strength and demanding independence.[[29]](#cite_note-37) Both Russia and Austria–Hungary saw an opportunity to expand in this region. Russia took on the role of protector of Slavs and Orthodox Christians. Austria envisioned a multi-ethnic, religiously diverse empire under Vienna's control. Count [Gyula Andrássy](/wiki/Gyula_Andr%C3%A1ssy "Gyula Andrássy"), a Hungarian who was Foreign Minister (1871–1879), made the centerpiece of his policy one of opposition to Russian expansion in the Balkans and blocking Serbian ambitions to dominate a new [South Slav](/wiki/South_Slavs "South Slavs") federation. He wanted Germany to ally with Austria, not Russia.[[30]](#cite_note-38)

### 1877–1908: Congress of Berlin and Balkan instability

Main articles: [Russo-Turkish War (1877–1878)](/wiki/Russo-Turkish_War_(1877%E2%80%931878) "Russo-Turkish War (1877–1878)"), [Congress of Berlin](/wiki/Congress_of_Berlin "Congress of Berlin"), and [Bosnia and Herzegovina in Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina_in_Austria-Hungary "Bosnia and Herzegovina in Austria-Hungary")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Archduke_Eugen_Bosniaks.jpg/250px-Archduke_Eugen_Bosniaks.jpg)](/wiki/File:Archduke_Eugen_Bosniaks.jpg)

Recruits from Bosnia-Herzegovina, including Muslim [Bosniaks](/wiki/Bosniaks "Bosniaks") (31%), were drafted into [special units](/wiki/Bosnian-Herzegovinian_Infantry "Bosnian-Herzegovinian Infantry") of the [Austro-Hungarian Army](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Army "Austro-Hungarian Army") as early as 1879 and were commended for their bravery in service of the Austrian emperor, being awarded more medals than any other unit. The military march "[Die Bosniaken kommen](/wiki/Die_Bosniaken_Kommen_(March) "Die Bosniaken Kommen (March)")" was composed in their honor by [Eduard Wagnes](/wiki/Eduard_Wagnes "Eduard Wagnes").[[31]](#cite_note-39)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Sarajevo_1878..jpg/250px-Sarajevo_1878..jpg)](/wiki/File:Sarajevo_1878..jpg)

Bosnian Muslim resistance during the battle of Sarajevo in 1878 against the [Austro-Hungarian occupation](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_campaign_in_Bosnia_and_Herzegovina_in_1878 "Austro-Hungarian campaign in Bosnia and Herzegovina in 1878")

Russian [Pan-Slavic](/wiki/Pan-Slavic "Pan-Slavic") organizations sent aid to the Balkan rebels and so pressured the tsar's government to declare war on the Ottoman Empire in 1877 in the name of protecting Orthodox Christians.[[26]](#cite_note-Kann_1974-34) Unable to mediate between the Ottoman Empire and Russia over the control of Serbia, Austria–Hungary declared neutrality when the conflict between the two powers escalated into a [war](/wiki/Russo-Turkish_War_(1877%E2%80%931878) "Russo-Turkish War (1877–1878)"). With help from Romania and Greece, Russia defeated the Ottomans and with the [Treaty of San Stefano](/wiki/Treaty_of_San_Stefano "Treaty of San Stefano") tried to create a large pro-Russian Bulgaria.

This treaty sparked an international uproar that almost resulted in a general European war. Austria–Hungary and Britain feared that a large Bulgaria would become a Russian satellite that would enable the tsar to dominate the Balkans. British prime minister [Benjamin Disraeli](/wiki/Benjamin_Disraeli "Benjamin Disraeli") moved warships into position against Russia to halt the advance of Russian influence in the eastern Mediterranean so close to Britain's route through the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal").[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlbrecht-Carrié1973chapter_6-40) The Treaty of San Stefano was seen in Austria as much too favourable for Russia and its Orthodox-Slavic goals. At the [Congress of Berlin](/wiki/Congress_of_Berlin "Congress of Berlin") in 1878 [Gyula Andrássy](/wiki/Gyula_Andr%C3%A1ssy "Gyula Andrássy") (Minister of Foreign Affairs), with British support, managed to force Russia to reduce its excessive demands in the Balkans. As a result, [Greater Bulgaria](/wiki/Greater_Bulgaria "Greater Bulgaria") was broken up and remained under nominal Ottoman control, Serbia, Montenegro and Romania became fully independent and Austria occupied (but did not annex) the provinces of Bosnia and Herzegovina, a predominantly Slavic area. The occupation required 150,000 troops and several weeks of fighting; the provinces were put under the administration of the Ministry of Finance.[[33]](#cite_note-B-41) In that year, with Britain's support, Austria–Hungary stationed troops in Bosnia to prevent the Russians from expanding into nearby Serbia. In another measure to keep the Russians out of the Balkans, Austria–Hungary formed an alliance, the Mediterranean Entente, with Britain and Italy in 1887 and concluded mutual defence pacts with Germany in 1879 and Romania in 1883 against a possible Russian attack.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAustria-Hungary_-_MSN_Encarta-42) Following the Congress of Berlin the European powers attempted to guarantee stability through a complex series of alliances and treaties.

Anxious about Balkan instability and Russian aggression, and to counter French interests in Europe, Austria–Hungary forged a [defensive alliance](/wiki/Defensive_alliance "Defensive alliance") with Germany in October 1879 and in May 1882. In October 1882 Italy joined this partnership in the [Triple Alliance](/wiki/Triple_Alliance_(1882) "Triple Alliance (1882)") largely because of Italy's imperial rivalries with France. Tensions between Russia and Austria–Hungary remained high, so Bismarck replaced the [League of the Three Emperors](/wiki/League_of_the_Three_Emperors "League of the Three Emperors") with the [Reinsurance Treaty](/wiki/Reinsurance_Treaty "Reinsurance Treaty") with Russia to keep the Habsburgs from recklessly starting a war over Pan-Slavism.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlbrecht-Carrié1973201–214-43) The [Sandžak-Raška / Novibazar](/wiki/Sand%C5%BEak "Sandžak") region was under Austro-Hungarian occupation between 1878 and 1909, when it was returned to the Ottoman Empire, before being ultimately divided between kingdoms of [Montenegro](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Montenegro "Kingdom of Montenegro") and [Serbia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbia "Kingdom of Serbia").[[36]](#cite_note-Mount_HolyOak-44)

### 1908: Bosnian Crisis

Main article: [Bosnia Crisis](/wiki/Bosnia_Crisis "Bosnia Crisis")

The monarchy eventually [annexed Bosnia and Herzegovina in October 1908](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_annexation_of_Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Austro-Hungarian annexation of Bosnia and Herzegovina") as a common holding of Cisleithania and Transleithania under the control of the [Imperial & Royal finance ministry](/w/index.php?title=Ministry_of_Finance_(Austria-Hungary)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ministry of Finance (Austria-Hungary) (page does not exist)") rather than attaching it to either territorial government. The annexation in 1908 led some in Vienna to contemplate combining Bosnia and Herzegovina with Croatia to form a third Slavic component of the monarchy. The deaths of Franz Joseph's brother, [Maximilian](/wiki/Maximilian_I_of_Mexico "Maximilian I of Mexico") (1867), and his only son, [Rudolf](/wiki/Rudolf,_Crown_Prince_of_Austria "Rudolf, Crown Prince of Austria"), made the Emperor's nephew, [Franz Ferdinand](/wiki/Archduke_Franz_Ferdinand_of_Austria "Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria"), heir to the throne. The Archduke was rumoured to have been an advocate for this trialism as a means to limit the power of the Hungarian aristocracy.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlbrecht-Carrié1973chapter_8-45)

A proclamation issued on the occasion of its annexation to the Habsburg monarchy in October 1908 promised these lands constitutional institutions, which should secure to their inhabitants full civil rights and a share in the management of their own affairs by means of a local representative assembly. In performance of this promise a constitution was promulgated in 1910.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlbrecht-Carrié1973259–272-46)

The principal players in the [Bosnian Crisis](/wiki/Bosnian_Crisis#Buchlau_Bargain "Bosnian Crisis") of 1908–09 were the foreign ministers of Austria and Russia, [Alois Lexa von Aehrenthal](/wiki/Alois_Lexa_von_Aehrenthal "Alois Lexa von Aehrenthal") and [Alexander Izvolsky](/wiki/Alexander_Izvolsky "Alexander Izvolsky"). Both were motivated by political ambition; the first would emerge successful, and the latter would be broken by the crisis. Along the way, they would drag Europe to the brink of war in 1909. They would also divide Europe into the two armed camps that would go to war in July 1914.[[39]](#cite_note-47)[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWank2020-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/KuK_Infanterie_1898.jpg/250px-KuK_Infanterie_1898.jpg)](/wiki/File:KuK_Infanterie_1898.jpg)

k.u.k. [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry") in 1898

Aehrenthal had started with the assumption that the Slavic minorities could never come together, and the Balkan League would never cause any damage to Austria. He turned down an Ottoman proposal for an alliance that would include Austria, Turkey, and Romania. However, his policies alienated the Bulgarians, who turned instead to Russia and Serbia. Although Austria had no intention to embark on additional expansion to the south, Aehrenthal encouraged speculation to that effect, expecting that it would paralyze the Balkan states. Instead, it incited them to feverish activity to create a defensive block to stop Austria. A series of grave miscalculations at the highest level thus significantly strengthened Austria's enemies.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBridge1972338–339-49)

In 1914, Slavic militants in Bosnia rejected Austria's plan to fully absorb the area; they [assassinated the Austrian heir](/wiki/Assassination_of_Archduke_Franz_Ferdinand_of_Austria "Assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria") and precipitated World War I.[[42]](#cite_note-50)

### 1914–1918: World War I

Main article: [History of Austria-Hungary during World War I](/wiki/History_of_Austria-Hungary_during_World_War_I "History of Austria-Hungary during World War I")

#### Prelude to World War I

Main articles: [July Crisis](/wiki/July_Crisis "July Crisis"), [Assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand](/wiki/Assassination_of_Archduke_Franz_Ferdinand "Assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand"), and [Austro-Hungarian entry into World War I](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_entry_into_World_War_I "Austro-Hungarian entry into World War I")

Further information: [Causes of World War I](/wiki/Causes_of_World_War_I "Causes of World War I")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Ferdinand_Behr_arrested_in_Sarajevo_1914.jpg/250px-Ferdinand_Behr_arrested_in_Sarajevo_1914.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ferdinand_Behr_arrested_in_Sarajevo_1914.jpg)

This picture of the [arrest of a suspect in Sarajevo](/wiki/Arrest_of_a_suspect_in_Sarajevo "Arrest of a suspect in Sarajevo") is usually associated with the capture of [Gavrilo Princip](/wiki/Gavrilo_Princip "Gavrilo Princip"), although some[[43]](#cite_note-FinestoneMassie-51)[[44]](#cite_note-OneMorningSarajevo-52) believe it depicts Ferdinand Behr, a bystander detained by mistake.

The 28 June 1914 assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in the Bosnian capital, [Sarajevo](/wiki/Sarajevo "Sarajevo"), excessively intensified the existing traditional religion-based ethnic hostilities in Bosnia. However, in Sarajevo itself, Austrian authorities encouraged[[45]](#cite_note-DjordjevićSpence1992-53)[[46]](#cite_note-54) violence against the Serb residents, which resulted in [anti-Serb riots in Sarajevo](/wiki/Anti-Serb_riots_of_Sarajevo "Anti-Serb riots of Sarajevo"), in which Catholic Croats and [Bosnian Muslims](/wiki/Bosnian_Muslims "Bosnian Muslims") killed two and damaged numerous Serb-owned buildings. Writer [Ivo Andrić](/wiki/Ivo_Andri%C4%87 "Ivo Andrić") referred to the violence as the "Sarajevo frenzy of hate."[[47]](#cite_note-Gioseffi1993-55) Violent actions against ethnic Serbs were organized not only in Sarajevo but also in many other larger Austro-Hungarian cities in modern-day Croatia and Bosnia and Herzegovina.[[48]](#cite_note-Mitrović2007-56) Austro-Hungarian authorities in Bosnia and Herzegovina imprisoned and extradited approximately 5,500 prominent Serbs, 700 to 2,200 of whom died in prison. Four hundred sixty Serbs were sentenced to death and a predominantly Muslim[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETomasevich2001485The_Bosnian_wartime_militia_(Schutzkorps),_which_became_known_for_its_persecution_of_Serbs,_was_overwhelmingly_Muslim.-57)[[50]](#cite_note-Schindler2007-58) special militia known as the *[Schutzkorps](/wiki/Schutzkorps "Schutzkorps")* was established and carried out the persecution of Serbs.[[51]](#cite_note-Kröll2008-59)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/1914-06-29_-_Aftermath_of_attacks_against_Serbs_in_Sarajevo.png/250px-1914-06-29_-_Aftermath_of_attacks_against_Serbs_in_Sarajevo.png)](/wiki/File:1914-06-29_-_Aftermath_of_attacks_against_Serbs_in_Sarajevo.png)

Crowds on the streets in the aftermath of the [Anti-Serb riots in Sarajevo](/wiki/Anti-Serb_riots_in_Sarajevo "Anti-Serb riots in Sarajevo"), 29 June 1914

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/M%C3%81V_armoured_train.jpg/250px-M%C3%81V_armoured_train.jpg)](/wiki/File:M%C3%81V_armoured_train.jpg)

[MÁVAG](/wiki/M%C3%81VAG "MÁVAG") armoured train in 1914

Some members of the government, such as Minister of Foreign Affairs [Count Leopold Berchtold](/wiki/Count_Leopold_Berchtold "Count Leopold Berchtold") and Army Commander [Count Franz Conrad von Hötzendorf](/wiki/Count_Franz_Conrad_von_H%C3%B6tzendorf "Count Franz Conrad von Hötzendorf"), had wanted to confront the resurgent Serbian nation for some years in a preventive war, but the Emperor and Hungarian prime minister [István Tisza](/wiki/Istv%C3%A1n_Tisza "István Tisza") were opposed. The foreign ministry of Austro-Hungarian Empire sent ambassador [László Szőgyény](/wiki/L%C3%A1szl%C3%B3_Sz%C5%91gy%C3%A9ny-Marich_Jr. "László Szőgyény-Marich Jr.") to [Potsdam](/wiki/Potsdam "Potsdam"), where he inquired about the standpoint of the German emperor, [Wilhelm II](/wiki/Wilhelm_II "Wilhelm II"), on 5 July and received a supportive response.

> His Majesty authorized me to report to [Franz Joseph] that in this case, too, we could count on Germany's full support. As mentioned, he first had to consult with the Chancellor, but he did not have the slightest doubt that [Herr von Bethmann Hollweg](/wiki/Theobald_von_Bethmann-Hollweg "Theobald von Bethmann-Hollweg") would fully agree with him, particularly with regard to action on our part against Serbia. In his [Wilhelm's] opinion, though, there was no need to wait patiently before taking action...[[52]](#cite_note-60)

The leaders of Austria–Hungary therefore decided to confront Serbia militarily before it could incite a revolt; using the assassination as an excuse, they presented a list of ten demands called the [July Ultimatum](/wiki/July_Ultimatum "July Ultimatum"),[[53]](#cite_note-firstworldwar-61) expecting Serbia would never accept. When Serbia accepted nine of the ten demands but only partially accepted the remaining one, Austria–Hungary declared war. Franz Joseph I finally followed the urgent counsel of his top advisers.

Over the course of July and August 1914, these events caused the start of World War I, as Russia mobilized in support of Serbia, setting off a series of counter-mobilizations. In support of his German ally, on Thursday, 6 August 1914, Emperor Franz Joseph signed the declaration of war on Russia. [Italy](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire") initially remained neutral, despite its alliance with Austria–Hungary. In 1915, it switched to the side of the [Entente powers](/wiki/Triple_Entente "Triple Entente"), hoping to gain territory from its former ally.[[54]](#cite_note-62)

#### Wartime foreign policy

Further information: [Diplomatic history of World War I](/wiki/Diplomatic_history_of_World_War_I "Diplomatic history of World War I")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Die_verb%C3%BCndeten_Monarchen_mit_ihren_Feldherren_im_1._Weltkrieg.jpg/250px-Die_verb%C3%BCndeten_Monarchen_mit_ihren_Feldherren_im_1._Weltkrieg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Die_verb%C3%BCndeten_Monarchen_mit_ihren_Feldherren_im_1._Weltkrieg.jpg)

[Franz Josef I](/wiki/Franz_Josef_I "Franz Josef I") and [Wilhelm II](/wiki/Wilhelm_II,_German_Emperor "Wilhelm II, German Emperor")  
with military commanders during World War I

The Austro-Hungarian Empire played a relatively passive diplomatic role in the war, as it was increasingly dominated and controlled by Germany.[[55]](#cite_note-63)[[56]](#cite_note-64) The only goal was to punish Serbia and try to stop the ethnic breakup of the Empire, and it completely failed. Starting in late 1916 the new Emperor Karl removed the pro-German officials and opened peace overtures to the Allies, whereby the entire war could be ended by compromise, or perhaps Austria would make a separate peace from Germany.[[57]](#cite_note-Stevenson,_1988_pp_139-65) The main effort was vetoed by Italy, which had been promised large slices of Austria for joining the Allies in 1915. Austria was only willing to turn over the Trentino region but nothing more.[[58]](#cite_note-66) Karl was seen as a defeatist, which weakened his standing at home and with both the Allies and [Germany](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire").[[59]](#cite_note-67)

#### Homefront

See also: [Hungary in World War I](/wiki/Hungary_in_World_War_I "Hungary in World War I")

The heavily rural empire did have a small industrial base, but its major contributions were manpower and food.[[60]](#cite_note-Schulze2005-68)[[61]](#cite_note-69) Nevertheless, Austria–Hungary was more urbanized (25%)[[62]](#cite_note-70) than its actual opponents in the war, like the Russian Empire (13.4%),[[63]](#cite_note-71) Serbia (13.2%)[[64]](#cite_note-72) or Romania (18.8%).[[65]](#cite_note-73) Furthermore, the Austro-Hungarian Empire had also more industrialized economy[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBroadberryO'Rourke2010[httpswebarchiveorgweb20151015202359httpsbooksgooglecombooksidYHk0z-ujS3ACpgPA70_70]-74) and higher GDP per capita[[67]](#cite_note-75) than the Kingdom of Italy, which was economically the far most developed actual opponent of the Empire.

On the home front, food grew scarcer and scarcer, as did heating fuel. Hungary, with its heavy agricultural base, was somewhat better fed. The army conquered productive agricultural areas in Romania and elsewhere, but refused to allow food shipments to civilians back home. Morale fell every year, and the diverse nationalities gave up on the empire and looked for ways to establish their own nation states.[[68]](#cite_note-76)

Inflation soared, from an index of 129 in 1914 to 1589 in 1918, wiping out the cash savings of the middle class. In terms of war damage to the economy, the war consumed about 20 percent of the [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product"). The dead soldiers amounted to about four percent of the 1914 labor force, and the wounded ones to another six percent. Compared to the major countries in the war, the death and casualty rates were toward the high end regarding the present-day territory of Austria.[[60]](#cite_note-Schulze2005-68)

By summer 1918, "[Green Cadres](/wiki/Green_Cadres "Green Cadres")" of army deserters formed armed bands in the hills of Croatia-Slavonia, and civil authority disintegrated. By late October, violence and massive looting erupted, and there were efforts to form peasant republics. However, the Croatian political leadership was focused on creating a new state (Yugoslavia) and worked with the advancing Serbian army to impose control and end the uprisings.[[69]](#cite_note-77)

#### Theaters of operations

See also: [Theater (warfare)](/wiki/Theater_(warfare) "Theater (warfare)")

The Austro-Hungarian Empire conscripted 7.8 million soldiers during the war.[[70]](#cite_note-78) General von Hötzendorf was the Chief of the Austro-Hungarian General Staff. Franz Joseph I, who was much too old to command the army, appointed [Archduke Friedrich von Österreich-Teschen](/wiki/Archduke_Friedrich,_Duke_of_Teschen "Archduke Friedrich, Duke of Teschen") as Supreme Army Commander (Armeeoberkommandant), but asked him to give Von Hötzendorf freedom to take any decisions. Von Hötzendorf remained in effective command of the military forces until Emperor Karl I took supreme command himself in late 1916 and dismissed Conrad von Hötzendorf in 1917. Meanwhile, economic conditions on the home front deteriorated rapidly. The empire depended on agriculture, and agriculture depended on the heavy labor of millions of men who were now in the army. Food production fell, the transportation system became overcrowded, and industrial production could not successfully handle the overwhelming need for munitions. Germany provided a great deal of help, but it was not enough. Furthermore, the political instability of the multiple ethnic groups within the empire now ripped apart any hope for national consensus in support of the war. Increasingly there was a demand for breaking up the empire and setting up autonomous national states based on historic, language-based cultures. The new emperor sought peace terms from the Allies, but his initiatives were vetoed by Italy.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWatson2014-79)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

##### Serbian front 1914–1916

Main articles: [Serbian Campaign (World War I)](/wiki/Serbian_Campaign_(World_War_I) "Serbian Campaign (World War I)") and [Austro-Hungarian occupation of Serbia](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_occupation_of_Serbia "Austro-Hungarian occupation of Serbia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/ShabatzBombardeadaOctubreDe1914--reportuponatroci00reis.jpg/250px-ShabatzBombardeadaOctubreDe1914--reportuponatroci00reis.jpg)](/wiki/File:ShabatzBombardeadaOctubreDe1914--reportuponatroci00reis.jpg)

[Šabac](/wiki/%C5%A0abac "Šabac") in Serbia, pictured in August 1914, was the first target of the [Austro-Hungarian punitive expedition](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_occupation_of_Serbia#Punitive_expedition_and_first_occupation "Austro-Hungarian occupation of Serbia") and the site of many [atrocities](/wiki/War_crimes_in_World_War_I "War crimes in World War I") committed against the local population.

At the start of the war, the army was divided into two: the smaller part attacked Serbia, while the larger part fought against the formidable [Imperial Russian Army](/wiki/Imperial_Russian_Army "Imperial Russian Army"). The invasion of Serbia in 1914 was a disaster: by the end of the year, the Austro-Hungarian Army had taken no territory, but had lost 227,000 out of a total force of 450,000 men. However, in the autumn of 1915, the Serbian Army was defeated by the Central Powers, which led to the occupation of Serbia. Near the end of 1915, in a massive rescue operation involving more than 1,000 trips made by Italian, French and British steamers, 260,000 Serb soldiers were transported to [Brindisi](/wiki/Brindisi "Brindisi") and [Corfu](/wiki/Corfu "Corfu"), where they waited for the chance of the victory of Allied powers to reclaim their country. Corfu hosted the Serbian government in exile after the collapse of Serbia and served as a supply base for the Greek front. In April 1916 a large number of Serbian troops were transported in British and French naval vessels from Corfu to mainland Greece. The contingent numbering over 120,000 relieved a much smaller army at the [Macedonian front](/wiki/Macedonian_front "Macedonian front") and fought alongside British and French troops.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrench_forces_occupy_Corfu_2011-80)

##### Russian front 1914–1917

Main article: [Eastern Front (World War I)](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_I) "Eastern Front (World War I)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Szturm_Twierdzy_Przemysl_A._Ritter_von_Meissl.jpg/250px-Szturm_Twierdzy_Przemysl_A._Ritter_von_Meissl.jpg)](/wiki/File:Szturm_Twierdzy_Przemysl_A._Ritter_von_Meissl.jpg)

[Siege of Przemyśl](/wiki/Siege_of_Przemy%C5%9Bl "Siege of Przemyśl") in 1915

On the [Eastern front](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_I) "Eastern Front (World War I)"), the war started out equally poorly. The government accepted the Polish proposal of establishing the [Supreme National Committee](/wiki/Supreme_National_Committee "Supreme National Committee") as the Polish central authority within the empire, responsible for the formation of the [Polish Legions](/wiki/Polish_Legions_in_World_War_I "Polish Legions in World War I"), an auxiliary military formation within the Austro-Hungarian Army. The Austro-Hungarian Army was defeated at the [Battle of Lemberg](/wiki/Battle_of_Galicia "Battle of Galicia") and the great fortress city of [Przemyśl was besieged](/wiki/Siege_of_Przemy%C5%9Bl "Siege of Przemyśl") and fell in March 1915. The [Gorlice–Tarnów Offensive](/wiki/Gorlice%E2%80%93Tarn%C3%B3w_Offensive "Gorlice–Tarnów Offensive") started as a minor German offensive to relieve the pressure of the Russian numerical superiority on the Austro-Hungarians, but the cooperation of the Central Powers resulted in huge Russian losses and the total collapse of the Russian lines and their 100 km (62 mi) long retreat into Russia. The Russian Third Army disintegrated. In summer 1915, the Austro-Hungarian Army, under a unified command with the Germans, participated in the successful Gorlice–Tarnów Offensive. From June 1916, the Russians focused their attacks on the Austro-Hungarian Army in the [Brusilov Offensive](/wiki/Brusilov_Offensive "Brusilov Offensive"), recognizing the latter's numerical inferiority. By the end of September 1916, Austria–Hungary mobilized and concentrated new divisions, and the successful Russian advance was halted and slowly repelled; but the Austrian armies took heavy losses (about 1 million men) and never recovered. Nevertheless, the huge losses in men and [materiel](/wiki/Materiel "Materiel") inflicted on the Russians during the offensive contributed greatly to the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution") of 1917 and caused an economic crash in the Russian Empire.

The [Act of 5 November](/wiki/Act_of_5_November "Act of 5 November") 1916 was then proclaimed to the [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") jointly by the Emperors [Wilhelm II](/wiki/Wilhelm_II_of_Germany "Wilhelm II of Germany") of Germany and Franz Joseph of Austria-Hungary. This act promised the creation of the [Kingdom of Poland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Poland_(1916-1918) "Kingdom of Poland (1916-1918)") out of the territory of [Congress Poland](/wiki/Congress_Poland "Congress Poland"), envisioned by its authors as a [puppet state](/wiki/Puppet_state "Puppet state") controlled by the Central Powers, with the nominal authority vested in the [Regency Council](/wiki/Regency_Council_(Poland) "Regency Council (Poland)"). The origin of that document was the dire need to draft new recruits from German-occupied Poland for the [war with Russia](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_I) "Eastern Front (World War I)"). Following the [Armistice of 11 November 1918](/wiki/Armistice_of_11_November_1918 "Armistice of 11 November 1918") ending the World War I, in spite of the previous initial total dependence of the kingdom on its sponsors, it ultimately served against their intentions as the cornerstone [proto state](/wiki/Proto_state "Proto state") of the nascent [Second Polish Republic](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic"), the latter composed also of territories never intended by the Central Powers to be ceded to Poland.

The [Battle of Zborov (1917)](/wiki/Battle_of_Zborov_(1917) "Battle of Zborov (1917)") was the first significant action of the [Czechoslovak Legions](/wiki/Czechoslovak_Legions "Czechoslovak Legions"), which fought for the independence of Czechoslovakia against the Austro-Hungarian Army.

##### Italian front 1915–1918

Main article: [Italian Front (World War I)](/wiki/Italian_Front_(World_War_I) "Italian Front (World War I)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Trento_3_novembre_1918.jpg/250px-Trento_3_novembre_1918.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trento_3_novembre_1918.jpg)

Italian troops in [Trento](/wiki/Trento "Trento") on 3 November 1918, after the [Battle of Vittorio Veneto](/wiki/Battle_of_Vittorio_Veneto "Battle of Vittorio Veneto"). Italy's victory marked the end of the war on the [Italian Front](/wiki/Italian_Front_(World_War_I) "Italian Front (World War I)") and secured the dissolution of Austria–Hungary.[[73]](#cite_note-81)

In May 1915, Italy attacked Austria–Hungary. Italy was the only military opponent of Austria–Hungary which had a similar degree of industrialization and economic level; moreover, her army was numerous (≈1,000,000 men were immediately fielded), but suffered from poor leadership, training and organization. [Chief of Staff](/wiki/Chief_of_Staff "Chief of Staff") [Luigi Cadorna](/wiki/Luigi_Cadorna "Luigi Cadorna") marched his army towards the [Isonzo](/wiki/Isonzo "Isonzo") River, hoping to seize Ljubljana, and to eventually threaten Vienna. However, the [Royal Italian Army](/wiki/Royal_Italian_Army "Royal Italian Army") were halted on the river, where [four battles](/wiki/Battles_of_the_Isonzo "Battles of the Isonzo") took place over five months (23 June – 2 December 1915). The fight was extremely bloody and exhausting for both sides.[[74]](#cite_note-82)

On 15 May 1916, Austrian Chief of Staff Conrad von Hötzendorf launched the *[Strafexpedition](/wiki/Battle_of_Asiago "Battle of Asiago")* ("[punitive expedition](/wiki/Punitive_expedition "Punitive expedition")"): the Austrians broke through the front and occupied the [Asiago plateau](/wiki/Asiago_plateau "Asiago plateau"). The Italians managed to resist and in a counteroffensive seized Gorizia on 9 August. Nonetheless, they had to stop on the [Carso](/wiki/Carso "Carso"), a few kilometres away from the border. At this point, several months of indecisive [trench warfare](/wiki/Trench_warfare "Trench warfare") ensued (analogous to the [Western front](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)")). As the Russian Empire collapsed as a result of the [Bolshevik Revolution](/wiki/Bolshevik_Revolution "Bolshevik Revolution") and the [Russians ended their involvement in the war](/wiki/Treaty_of_Brest-Litovsk_(Russia%E2%80%93Central_Powers) "Treaty of Brest-Litovsk (Russia–Central Powers)"), Germans and Austrians were able to transfer much of their manpower to the Western and Southern fronts from the erstwhile Eastern fighting.

On 24 October 1917, Austrians (now enjoying decisive German support) attacked at [Caporetto](/wiki/Battle_of_Caporetto "Battle of Caporetto") using new infiltration tactics; although they advanced more than 100 km (62.14 mi) in the direction of Venice and gained considerable supplies, they were halted and could not cross the [Piave](/wiki/Piave_(river) "Piave (river)") River. Italy, although suffering massive casualties, recovered from the blow, and a [coalition government](/wiki/Coalition_government "Coalition government") under [Vittorio Emanuele Orlando](/wiki/Vittorio_Emanuele_Orlando "Vittorio Emanuele Orlando") was formed. Italy also enjoyed the support of the Entente: by 1918, large amounts of war materials and a few auxiliary American, British, and French divisions arrived in the Italian battle zone.[[75]](#cite_note-83) Cadorna was replaced by General [Armando Diaz](/wiki/Armando_Diaz "Armando Diaz"); under his command, the Italians retook the initiative and won the decisive [Second Battle of the Piave River](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_the_Piave_River "Second Battle of the Piave River") (15–23 June 1918), in which some 60,000 Austrian and 43,000 Italian soldiers were killed. The final battle at [Vittorio Veneto](/wiki/Battle_of_Vittorio_Veneto "Battle of Vittorio Veneto") was lost by 31 October 1918 and the armistice was signed at [Villa Giusti](/wiki/Armistice_of_Villa_Giusti "Armistice of Villa Giusti") on 3 November.[[76]](#cite_note-84)

##### Romanian front 1916–1917

Main article: [Romania during World War I](/wiki/Romania_during_World_War_I "Romania during World War I")

On 27 August 1916, Romania declared war against Austria–Hungary. The [Romanian Army](/wiki/Romanian_Land_Forces "Romanian Land Forces") crossed the borders of eastern Hungary (Transylvania), but despite initial successes, by November 1916, the Austro-Hungarian, German, Bulgarian, and Ottoman armies had defeated the Romanian and Russian armies, and occupied the southern part of Romania (including [Oltenia](/wiki/Oltenia "Oltenia"), [Muntenia](/wiki/Muntenia "Muntenia") and [Dobruja](/wiki/Dobruja "Dobruja")). Within three months of the war, the Central Powers approached [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest"), the Romanian capital. On 6 December, Bucharest was captured, and part of the population moved to the unoccupied Romanian territory, in [Moldavia](/wiki/Western_Moldavia "Western Moldavia"), together with the Romanian government, royal court and public authorities, which relocated to [Iași](/wiki/Ia%C8%99i "Iași").[[77]](#cite_note-85) In 1917, after several defensive victories (managing to stop the German-Austro-Hungarian advance), with Russia's withdrawal from the war following the October Revolution, Romania was forced to drop out of the war.[[78]](#cite_note-86)

#### Role of Hungary

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Cs%C3%ADkp%C3%A1lfalva2.JPG/250px-Cs%C3%ADkp%C3%A1lfalva2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cs%C3%ADkp%C3%A1lfalva2.JPG)

War memorial in [Păuleni-Ciuc](/wiki/P%C4%83uleni-Ciuc "Păuleni-Ciuc"), Romania

Although the Kingdom of Hungary comprised only 42% of the population of Austria–Hungary,[[79]](#cite_note-87) the thin majority— more than 3.8 million soldiers—of the Austro-Hungarian armed forces were conscripted from the Kingdom of Hungary during the First World War. Roughly 600,000 soldiers were killed in action, and 700,000 soldiers were wounded in the war.[[80]](#cite_note-88)

Austria–Hungary held on for years, as the Hungarian half provided sufficient supplies for the military to continue to wage war.[[33]](#cite_note-B-41) This was shown in a transition of power after which the Hungarian prime minister, Count István Tisza, and foreign minister, Count [István Burián](/wiki/Istv%C3%A1n_Buri%C3%A1n "István Burián"), had decisive influence over the internal and external affairs of the monarchy.[[33]](#cite_note-B-41) By late 1916, food supply from Hungary became intermittent and the government sought an armistice with the Entente powers. However, this failed as Britain and France no longer had any regard for the integrity of the monarchy due to Austro-Hungarian support for Germany.[[33]](#cite_note-B-41)

#### Analysis of defeat

The setbacks that the Austrian army suffered in 1914 and 1915 can be attributed to a large extent by the incompetence of the Austrian high command.[[33]](#cite_note-B-41) After attacking Serbia, its forces soon had to be withdrawn to protect its eastern frontier against Russia's invasion, while German units were engaged in fighting on the Western Front. This resulted in a greater than expected loss of men in the invasion of Serbia.[[33]](#cite_note-B-41) Furthermore, it became evident that the Austrian high command had had no plans for possible continental war and that the army and navy were also ill-equipped to handle such a conflict.[[33]](#cite_note-B-41)

In the last two years of the war the Austro-Hungarian armed forces lost all ability to act independently of Germany. As of 7 September 1916, the German emperor was given full control of all the armed forces of the Central Powers and Austria-Hungary effectively became a satellite of Germany.[[81]](#cite_note-89) The Austrians viewed the [German army](/wiki/German_Army_(German_Empire) "German Army (German Empire)") favorably; on the other hand, by 1916 the general belief in Germany was that Germany, in its alliance with Austria–Hungary, was "shackled to a corpse". The operational capability of the Austro-Hungarian army was seriously affected by supply shortages, low morale and a high casualty rate, and by the army's composition of multiple ethnicities with different languages and customs.[[82]](#cite_note-90)

### 1918: Demise, disintegration, dissolution

Main article: [Dissolution of Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Dissolution_of_Austria-Hungary "Dissolution of Austria-Hungary")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Karl_v._Habsburg_5_Kingdoms-Oct.16_1918_%28v2%29.png/250px-Karl_v._Habsburg_5_Kingdoms-Oct.16_1918_%28v2%29.png)](/wiki/File:Karl_v._Habsburg_5_Kingdoms-Oct.16_1918_(v2).png)

[Karl I of Austria](/wiki/Charles_I_of_Austria "Charles I of Austria") envisaged the Habsburg Empire as being made up of five Kingdoms, in a last desperate attempt to save the Monarchy.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Austria_obituary.jpg/250px-Austria_obituary.jpg)](/wiki/File:Austria_obituary.jpg)

A humorous "[obituary](/wiki/Obituary "Obituary")" of the Austrian Empire, published in [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków") in late 1918. Translation:  
"Overwhelmed with joy, until recently enslaved and now free Nations announce that their wicked step-mother AUSTRIA the WITCH, operated in [William's](/wiki/Wilhelm_II_of_Germany "Wilhelm II of Germany") Clinic, died from senility after severe spasms, cursed by all those who had the infelicity of having to do with her. Her dreadful funeral took place during these days in the fields of Macedonia, on the [Piave River](/wiki/Piave_River "Piave River") and across the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine"). Let her rest in eternal peace and may she never raise from the dead.  
 [POLES](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people"), [CZECHO](/wiki/Czechs "Czechs")-[SLOVAKS](/wiki/Slovaks "Slovaks"), [YUGOSLAVS](/wiki/Yugoslavs "Yugoslavs")  
 Funeral home [Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") & Co."

By 1918, the economic situation had deteriorated and governmental failure on the homefront ended popular support for the war.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWatson2014536-91) The Austro-Hungarian monarchy collapsed with dramatic speed in the autumn of 1918. Leftist and pacifist political movements organized strikes in factories, and uprisings in the army had become commonplace.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWatson2014536–540-92) As the war went on, the ethnic unity declined; the Allies encouraged breakaway demands from minorities and the Empire faced disintegration.[[57]](#cite_note-Stevenson,_1988_pp_139-65) With apparent Allied victory approaching, nationalist movements seized ethnic resentment to erode social unity. The military breakdown of the Italian front marked the start of the rebellion for the numerous ethnicities who made up the multiethnic Empire, as they refused to keep on fighting for a cause that now appeared senseless. The Emperor had lost much of his power to rule, as his realm disintegrated.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWatson2014536–540-92)

On 14 October 1918, Foreign Minister Baron István Burián von Rajecz[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENagy2006-93) asked for an armistice based on President [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson")'s [Fourteen Points](/wiki/Fourteen_Points "Fourteen Points") and two days later Emperor Karl I issued a proclamation ("Imperial Manifesto of 16 October 1918") altering the empire into a federal union to give ethnic groups decentralization and representation.[[86]](#cite_note-94) However, on 18 October, United States Secretary of State [Robert Lansing](/wiki/Robert_Lansing "Robert Lansing") replied that autonomy for the nationalities – the tenth of the Fourteen Points – was no longer enough. In fact, a [Czechoslovak provisional government](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") had joined the Allies on 14 October. The South Slavs in both halves of the monarchy had already declared in favor of uniting with Serbia in a large South Slav state in the 1917 [Corfu Declaration](/wiki/Corfu_Declaration "Corfu Declaration") signed by members of the [Yugoslav Committee](/wiki/Yugoslav_Committee "Yugoslav Committee"). The Croatians had begun disregarding orders from Budapest earlier in October. Lansing's response was, in effect, the death certificate of Austria–Hungary.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENagy2006-93)[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWatson2014541–542-95)

During the Italian battles, the Czechoslovaks and Southern Slavs declared their independence. With defeat in the war imminent after the Italian offensive in the Battle of Vittorio Veneto on 24 October, Czech politicians peacefully took over command in Prague on 28 October (later declared the birth of Czechoslovakia) and followed up in other major cities in the next few days. On 30 October, the Slovaks did the same. On 29 October, the Slavs in both portions of what remained of Austria–Hungary proclaimed the [State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs](/wiki/State_of_Slovenes,_Croats_and_Serbs "State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs") and declared that their ultimate intention was to unite with Serbia and Montenegro in [a large South Slav state](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia"). On the same day, the Czechs and Slovaks formally proclaimed the establishment of Czechoslovakia as an independent state.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On 17 October 1918, the [Hungarian Parliament](/wiki/Hungarian_Parliament "Hungarian Parliament") voted in favour of terminating the union with Austria. The most prominent opponent of continued union with Austria, Count [Mihály Károlyi](/wiki/Mih%C3%A1ly_K%C3%A1rolyi "Mihály Károlyi"), seized power in the [Aster Revolution](/wiki/Aster_Revolution "Aster Revolution") on 31 October. Charles was all but forced to appoint Károlyi as his Hungarian prime minister. One of Károlyi's first acts was to formally repudiate the compromise agreement on 31 October, effectively terminating the personal union with Austria and thus officially dissolving the Austro-Hungarian state.[[88]](#cite_note-96)

By the end of October, there was nothing left of the Habsburg realm but its majority-German Danubian and Alpine provinces, and Karl's authority was being challenged even there by the German-Austrian state council.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWatson2014542–556-97) Karl's last Austrian prime minister, [Heinrich Lammasch](/wiki/Heinrich_Lammasch "Heinrich Lammasch"), concluded that Karl's position was untenable. Lammasch persuaded Karl that the best course was to relinquish, at least temporarily, his right to exercise sovereign authority. On 11 November, Karl issued a carefully worded proclamation in which he recognized the Austrian people's right to determine the form of the state and "relinquish(ed) every participation" in Austrian state affairs.[[90]](#cite_note-98) On the day after he announced his withdrawal from Austrian politics, the German-Austrian National Council proclaimed the [Republic of German Austria](/wiki/Republic_of_German_Austria "Republic of German Austria"). Károlyi followed suit on 16 November, proclaiming the [Hungarian Democratic Republic](/wiki/Hungarian_Democratic_Republic "Hungarian Democratic Republic").

## Government

Main article: [Government of Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Government_of_Austria-Hungary "Government of Austria-Hungary")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Parliament_Buildung_Hungary_20090920.jpg/250px-Parliament_Buildung_Hungary_20090920.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parliament_Buildung_Hungary_20090920.jpg)

Hungarian Parliament building

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Parlament_Wien_abends_edit.jpg/250px-Parlament_Wien_abends_edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parlament_Wien_abends_edit.jpg)

Austrian Parliament building

The [Compromise of 1867](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Compromise_of_1867 "Austro-Hungarian Compromise of 1867") turned the [Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg-Lorraine "House of Habsburg-Lorraine") domains into a [real union](/wiki/Real_union "Real union") between the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") ("Lands Represented in the Imperial Council", or [Cisleithania](/wiki/Cisleithania "Cisleithania"))[[7]](#cite_note-ah1911-13) in the western and northern half and the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary") ("Lands of the Crown of Saint Stephen", or [Transleithania](/wiki/Transleithania "Transleithania")) in the eastern half.[[7]](#cite_note-ah1911-13)

The government of Austria, which had ruled the monarchy until 1867, became the government of the Austrian part, and another government was formed for the Hungarian part. The common government (officially designated Ministerial Council for Common Affairs, or *Ministerrat für gemeinsame Angelegenheiten* in German) formed for the few matters of common national security - the [Common Army](/wiki/Common_Army "Common Army"), [Navy](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Navy "Austro-Hungarian Navy"), foreign policy and the imperial household, and the customs union.[[26]](#cite_note-Kann_1974-34) Although the two halves shared a common monarch and both foreign relations and defense were managed jointly, all other state functions were to be handled separately as there was no common citizenship.[[i]](#cite_note-100)[[7]](#cite_note-ah1911-13)[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoman2003401-101)[[93]](#cite_note-102)

Hungary and Austria maintained separate [parliaments](/wiki/Parliament "Parliament"), each with its own [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_minister "Prime minister"): the [Diet of Hungary](/wiki/Diet_of_Hungary "Diet of Hungary") (commonly known as the National Assembly) and the [Imperial Council](/wiki/Imperial_Council_(Austria) "Imperial Council (Austria)") ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Reichsrat*) in Cisleithania. Each parliament had its own executive government, appointed by the monarch.[[22]](#cite_note-:0-30)[[94]](#cite_note-103)

After 1878, [Bosnia and Herzegovina came under Austro-Hungarian military and civilian rule](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_rule_in_Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Austro-Hungarian rule in Bosnia and Herzegovina")[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMinahan1998[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidRSxt-JB-PDkCpgPA48_48]-24) until it was fully annexed in 1908, provoking the [Bosnian crisis](/wiki/Bosnian_crisis "Bosnian crisis") with the Great Powers and Austria-Hungary's Balkan neighbors, [Serbia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbia "Kingdom of Serbia") and [Montenegro](/wiki/Principality_of_Montenegro "Principality of Montenegro").[[17]](#cite_note-b1911-25)

Relations during the half-century after 1867 between the two parts of the dual monarchy featured repeated disputes over shared external tariff arrangements and over the financial contribution of each government to the common treasury. These matters were determined by the Austro-Hungarian Compromise of 1867, in which common expenditures were allocated 70% to Austria and 30% to Hungary. This division had to be renegotiated every ten years. There was political turmoil during the build-up to each renewal of the agreement. By 1907, the Hungarian share had risen to 36.4%.[[95]](#cite_note-kronenbitter-104) The disputes culminated in the early 1900s in a prolonged [constitutional crisis](/wiki/Constitutional_crisis "Constitutional crisis"). It was triggered by disagreement over which language to use for command in [Hungarian army](/wiki/Military_of_Hungary "Military of Hungary") units and deepened by the advent to power in Budapest in April 1906 of a Hungarian nationalist coalition. Provisional renewals of the common arrangements occurred in October 1907 and in November 1917 on the basis of the *status quo*. The negotiations in 1917 ended with the dissolution of the Dual Monarchy.[[96]](#cite_note-Taylor_1964-105)

## Demographics

Main article: [Ethnic and religious composition of Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Ethnic_and_religious_composition_of_Austria-Hungary "Ethnic and religious composition of Austria-Hungary")

See also: [Trialism in Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Trialism_in_Austria-Hungary "Trialism in Austria-Hungary"), [United States of Greater Austria](/wiki/United_States_of_Greater_Austria "United States of Greater Austria"), [Magyarization](/wiki/Magyarization "Magyarization"), [Austro-Slavism](/wiki/Austro-Slavism "Austro-Slavism"), and [Panslavism](/wiki/Panslavism "Panslavism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Austria_Hungary_ethnic.svg/500px-Austria_Hungary_ethnic.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Austria_Hungary_ethnic.svg)

Ethno-linguistic map of Austria-Hungary, 1910

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Meyers_b12_s0486a.jpg/500px-Meyers_b12_s0486a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meyers_b12_s0486a.jpg)

*[Meyers Konversations-Lexikon](/wiki/Meyers_Konversations-Lexikon "Meyers Konversations-Lexikon")* ethnographic map of Austria-Hungary, 1885

In July 1849, the Hungarian Revolutionary Parliament proclaimed and enacted ethnic and minority rights (the next such laws were in Switzerland), but these were overturned after the Russian and Austrian armies crushed the Hungarian Revolution. After the Kingdom of Hungary reached the Compromise with the Habsburg Dynasty in 1867, one of the first acts of its restored Parliament was to pass a Law on Nationalities (Act Number XLIV of 1868). It was a liberal piece of legislation and offered extensive language and cultural rights. It did not recognize non-Hungarians to have rights to form states with any territorial autonomy.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGábór2010-106)

Article 19 of the 1867 "Basic State Act" (*Staatsgrundgesetz*), valid only for the Cisleithanian (Austrian) part of Austria–Hungary,[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStaatsgrundgesetz_1867-107) said:

> All races of the empire have equal rights, and every race has an inviolable right to the preservation and use of its own nationality and language. The equality of all customary languages ("*landesübliche Sprachen*") in school, office and public life, is recognized by the state. In those territories in which several races dwell, the public and educational institutions are to be so arranged that, without applying compulsion to learn a second country language ("*Landessprache*"), each of the races receives the necessary means of education in its own language.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeadlam191139-108)

The implementation of this principle led to several disputes, as it was not clear which languages could be regarded as "customary". The Germans, the traditional bureaucratic, capitalist and cultural elite, demanded the recognition of their language as a customary language in every part of the empire. German nationalists, especially in the [Sudetenland](/wiki/Sudetenland "Sudetenland") (part of Bohemia), looked to Berlin in the new German Empire.[[100]](#cite_note-109)

The Hungarian Minority Act of 1868 gave the minorities (Slovaks, Romanians, Serbs, et al.) individual (but not also communal) rights to use their language in offices, schools (although in practice often only in those founded by them and not by the state), courts and municipalities (if 20% of the deputies demanded it). Beginning with the 1879 Primary Education Act and the 1883 Secondary Education Act, the Hungarian state made more efforts to reduce the use of non-Magyar languages, in strong violation of the 1868 Nationalities Law.[[101]](#cite_note-110) After 1875, all Slovak language schools higher than elementary were closed, including the only three high schools [(gymnasiums)](/wiki/Gymnasium_(school) "Gymnasium (school)") in [Revúca](/wiki/Rev%C3%BAca "Revúca") (Nagyrőce), [Turčiansky Svätý Martin](/wiki/Martin,_Slovakia "Martin, Slovakia") (Turócszentmárton) and [Kláštor pod Znievom](/wiki/Kl%C3%A1%C5%A1tor_pod_Znievom "Kláštor pod Znievom") (Znióváralja).

Language was, as a proxy for ethnicity, one of the most contentious issues in Austro-Hungarian politics. All governments faced difficult and divisive hurdles in deciding on the languages of government and of instruction. The minorities sought the widest opportunities for education in their own languages, as well as in the "dominant" languages—Hungarian and German. By the "Ordinance of 5 April 1897", the Austrian Prime Minister [Count Kasimir Felix Badeni](/wiki/Count_Kasimir_Felix_Badeni "Count Kasimir Felix Badeni") gave Czech equal standing with German in the internal government of Bohemia; this led to a crisis because of nationalist German agitation throughout the empire. The Crown dismissed Badeni.[[102]](#cite_note-111)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Austria1914physical.jpg/500px-Austria1914physical.jpg)](/wiki/File:Austria1914physical.jpg)

[Topographic](/wiki/Topographic "Topographic") map of Austria-Hungary in 1914

Italian was regarded as an old "culture language" (**Kultursprache**) by German intellectuals and had always been granted [equal rights](/wiki/Social_equality "Social equality") as an [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of the Empire, but the Germans had difficulty in accepting the [Slavic languages](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages") as equal to their own. On one occasion [Count A. Auersperg](/wiki/Count_Anton_Alexander_von_Auersperg "Count Anton Alexander von Auersperg") (Anastasius Grün) entered the Diet of [Carniola](/wiki/Carniola "Carniola") carrying what he claimed to be the whole [corpus](/wiki/Text_corpus "Text corpus") of [Slovene literature](/wiki/Slovene_literature "Slovene literature") under his arm; this was to demonstrate that the [Slovene language](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language") could not be substituted for German as the language of higher education.

The following years saw official recognition of several languages, at least in Austria. Since 1867, laws awarded Croatian equal status with Italian in [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia"). Beginning in 1882, there was a Slovene majority in the Diet of Carniola and in the capital [Laibach (Ljubljana)](/wiki/Ljubljana "Ljubljana"); they replaced German with Slovene as their primary official language. [Galicia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia_and_Lodomeria "Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria") designated Polish instead of German in 1869 as the customary language of government.[[103]](#cite_note-112)

As of June 1907, all public and [private schools](/wiki/Private_school "Private school") in Hungary were obliged to ensure that [after the fourth grade, the pupils could express themselves fluently in Hungarian](/wiki/Magyarization "Magyarization"). This led to the further closing of minority schools, devoted mostly to the Slovak and Rusyn languages. The two kingdoms sometimes divided their [spheres of influence](/wiki/Sphere_of_influence "Sphere of influence"). According to [Misha Glenny](/wiki/Misha_Glenny "Misha Glenny") in his book, *The Balkans, 1804–1999*, the Austrians responded to Hungarian support of Czechs by supporting the Croatian national movement in Zagreb. In recognition that he reigned in a multi-ethnic country, Emperor Franz Joseph spoke (and used) German, Hungarian and Czech fluently, and Croatian, Serbian, Polish and Italian to some degree.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Nagy_G%C3%A9za-Nemes_Mih%C3%A1ly_A_magyar_viseletek_t%C3%B6rt%C3%A9nete_1900.jpg/250px-Nagy_G%C3%A9za-Nemes_Mih%C3%A1ly_A_magyar_viseletek_t%C3%B6rt%C3%A9nete_1900.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagy_G%C3%A9za-Nemes_Mih%C3%A1ly_A_magyar_viseletek_t%C3%B6rt%C3%A9nete_1900.jpg)

Traditional clothing of various [ethnic groups in Hungary](/wiki/Demographics_of_the_Kingdom_of_Hungary_by_county "Demographics of the Kingdom of Hungary by county"), late 19th century

The language disputes were most fiercely fought in Bohemia, where the Czech speakers formed a majority and sought equal status for their language to German. The [Czechs](/wiki/Czechs "Czechs") had lived primarily in Bohemia since the 6th century and German immigrants had begun settling the Bohemian periphery in the 13th century. The constitution of 1627 made the German language a second official language and equal to Czech. German speakers lost their majority in the Bohemian Diet in 1880 and became a minority to Czech speakers in the cities of [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague") and [Pilsen](/wiki/Plze%C5%88 "Plzeň") (while retaining a slight numerical majority in the city of [Brno (Brünn)](/wiki/Brno "Brno")). The old [Charles University in Prague](/wiki/Charles_University_in_Prague "Charles University in Prague"), hitherto dominated by German speakers, was divided into German and Czech-speaking faculties in 1882.[[104]](#cite_note-113)

At the same time, Hungarian dominance faced challenges from majority populated areas of [Romanians](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians"), [Slovaks](/wiki/Slovaks "Slovaks") and [Serbs](/wiki/Serbs "Serbs"). The Romanians and the Serbs began to agitate for union with their fellow nationalists and language speakers in the newly founded states of [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") (1859–1878) and Serbia.[[105]](#cite_note-114)[[106]](#cite_note-115)[[107]](#cite_note-116)[[108]](#cite_note-117)[[109]](#cite_note-118)[[110]](#cite_note-119)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Emil_Rau_-_A_Summer%E2%80%99s_Day_in_Tyrol.jpg/250px-Emil_Rau_-_A_Summer%E2%80%99s_Day_in_Tyrol.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emil_Rau_-_A_Summer%E2%80%99s_Day_in_Tyrol.jpg)

Traditional costumes of [Tyrol](/wiki/Tyrol "Tyrol")

Hungary's leaders were generally less willing than their Austrian counterparts to share power with their subject minorities, but they granted a large measure of autonomy to Croatia in 1868. To some extent, they modeled their relationship to that kingdom on their own compromise with Austria of the previous year. In spite of nominal autonomy, the Croatian government was an economic and administrative part of Hungary, which the Croatians resented. In the [Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Croatia-Slavonia "Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia") and Bosnia and Herzegovina many advocated the idea of a [trialist Austro-Hungaro-Croatian](/wiki/Trialism_in_Austria-Hungary "Trialism in Austria-Hungary") monarchy; among the supporters of the idea were [Archduke Leopold Salvator](/wiki/Archduke_Leopold_Salvator_of_Austria "Archduke Leopold Salvator of Austria"), Archduke Franz Ferdinand and emperor and king [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_Austria "Charles I of Austria") who during his short reign supported the trialist idea only to be vetoed by the Hungarian government and Count [István Tisza](/wiki/Istv%C3%A1n_Tisza "István Tisza"). The count finally signed the trialist proclamation after heavy pressure from the king on 23 October 1918.[[111]](#cite_note-Budisavljević-120)

### Ethnic relations

In [Istria](/wiki/Margraviate_of_Istria "Margraviate of Istria"), the [Istro-Romanians](/wiki/Istro-Romanians "Istro-Romanians"), a small ethnic group composed by around 2,600 people in the 1880s,[[112]](#cite_note-121) suffered severe discrimination. The Croats of the region, who formed the majority, tried to assimilate them, while the Italian minority supported them in their requests for self-determination.[[113]](#cite_note-122)[[114]](#cite_note-123) In 1888, the possibility of opening the first school for the Istro-Romanians teaching in the Romanian language was discussed in the [Diet of Istria](/wiki/Diet_of_Istria "Diet of Istria"). The proposal was very popular among them. The Italian [deputies](/wiki/Deputy_(legislator) "Deputy (legislator)") showed their support, but the Croat ones opposed it and tried to show that the Istro-Romanians were in fact Slavs.[[115]](#cite_note-124) During Austro-Hungarian rule, the Istro-Romanians lived under [poverty](/wiki/Poverty "Poverty") conditions,[[116]](#cite_note-istro-125) and those living in the island of [Krk](/wiki/Krk "Krk") were fully assimilated by 1875.[[117]](#cite_note-126)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/02_Pecs%2C_Hungary_-_Great_Synagogue.jpg/250px-02_Pecs%2C_Hungary_-_Great_Synagogue.jpg)](/wiki/File:02_Pecs,_Hungary_-_Great_Synagogue.jpg)

[Romantic-style](/wiki/Romantic_architecture "Romantic architecture") Great Synagogue in [Pécs](/wiki/P%C3%A9cs "Pécs"), built by the [Neolog Jewish community](/wiki/Neolog_Judaism "Neolog Judaism") in 1869

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Orthodox_Jews_in_Leopoldstadt_1915.JPG/250px-Orthodox_Jews_in_Leopoldstadt_1915.JPG)](/wiki/File:Orthodox_Jews_in_Leopoldstadt_1915.JPG)

Orthodox Jews from [Galicia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia_and_Lodomeria "Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria") in [Leopoldstadt](/wiki/Leopoldstadt "Leopoldstadt"), Vienna, 1915

Around 1900, Jews numbered about two million in the whole territory of the Austro-Hungarian Empire;[[118]](#cite_note-127) their position was ambiguous. The [populist](/wiki/Populism "Populism") and [antisemitic](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism") politics of the [Christian Social Party](/wiki/Christian_Social_Party_(Austria) "Christian Social Party (Austria)") are sometimes viewed as a model for [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler")'s [Nazism](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism").[[119]](#cite_note-128) Antisemitic parties and movements existed, but the governments of Vienna and Budapest did not initiate [pogroms](/wiki/Pogroms "Pogroms") or implement official antisemitic policies.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] They feared that such [ethnic violence](/wiki/Ethnic_violence "Ethnic violence") could ignite other [ethnic minorities](/wiki/Minority_group "Minority group") and escalate out of control. The antisemitic parties remained on the periphery of the political sphere due to their low popularity among voters in the parliamentary elections.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In that period, the majority of Jews in Austria–Hungary lived in small towns (*[shtetls](/wiki/Shtetls "Shtetls")*) in [Galicia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia_and_Lodomeria "Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria") and rural areas in Hungary and Bohemia; however, they had large communities and even local majorities in the downtown districts of Vienna, Budapest, Prague, Kraków and [Lwów](/wiki/Lw%C3%B3w "Lwów"). Of the pre-World War I military forces of the major European powers, the Austro-Hungarian army was almost alone in its regular promotion of Jews to positions of command.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERothenberg1976118-129) While the Jewish population of the lands of the Dual Monarchy was about 5%, Jews made up nearly 18% of the reserve officer corps.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERothenberg1976128-130) Thanks to the modernity of the constitution and to the benevolence of emperor Franz Joseph, the Austrian Jews came to regard the era of Austria–Hungary as a golden era of their history.[[122]](#cite_note-131) By 1910 about 900,000 religious[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Jews made up approximately 5% of the population of Hungary and about 23% of Budapest's citizenry. In the Austro-Hungarian Empire the generally fiercely patriotic Hungarian Jews were securing the tenuous Hungarian majority in the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary").[[j]](#cite_note-133) Jews accounted for 54% of commercial business owners, 85% of financial institution directors and owners in banking, and 62% of all employees in commerce,[[124]](#cite_note-134) 20% of all general grammar school students, and 37% of all commercial scientific grammar school students, 31.9% of all engineering students, and 34.1% of all students in human faculties of the universities. Jews accounted for 48.5% of all physicians,[[125]](#cite_note-135) and 49.4% of all lawyers/jurists in Hungary.[[126]](#cite_note-136) Note: The numbers of Jews were reconstructed from religious censuses. They did not include the people of Jewish origin who had converted to Christianity, or the number of atheists.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Among many Hungarian parliament members of Jewish origin, the most famous Jewish members in Hungarian political life were Minister of Justice [Vilmos Vázsonyi](/wiki/Vilmos_V%C3%A1zsonyi "Vilmos Vázsonyi"), Minister of War [Samu Hazai](/wiki/Samu_Hazai "Samu Hazai"), Minister of Finance [János Teleszky](/wiki/J%C3%A1nos_Teleszky "János Teleszky"), and ministers of trade [János Harkányi](/w/index.php?title=J%C3%A1nos_Hark%C3%A1nyi&action=edit&redlink=1 "János Harkányi (page does not exist)") [[hu](https://hu.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hark%C3%A1nyi_J%C3%A1nos_(politikus) "hu:Harkányi János (politikus)")] and [József Szterényi](/w/index.php?title=J%C3%B3zsef_Szter%C3%A9nyi&action=edit&redlink=1 "József Szterényi (page does not exist)") [[hu](https://hu.wikipedia.org/wiki/Szter%C3%A9nyi_J%C3%B3zsef "hu:Szterényi József")].

### Universities in Cisleithania

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Literacy_in_Austria-Hungary_%281880%29.JPG/500px-Literacy_in_Austria-Hungary_%281880%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Literacy_in_Austria-Hungary_(1880).JPG)

Literacy in Austria-Hungary (census 1880)

The first university in the Austrian half of the Empire ([Charles University](/wiki/Charles_University "Charles University")) was founded by [Emperor Charles IV](/wiki/Charles_IV,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles IV, Holy Roman Emperor") in Prague in 1347, the second oldest university was the [Jagiellonian University](/wiki/Jagiellonian_University "Jagiellonian University") established in [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków") by the King of Poland [Casimir III the Great](/wiki/Casimir_III_the_Great "Casimir III the Great") in 1364, while the third oldest ([University of Vienna](/wiki/University_of_Vienna "University of Vienna")) was founded by [Duke Rudolph IV](/wiki/Rudolf_IV,_Duke_of_Austria "Rudolf IV, Duke of Austria") in 1365.[[127]](#cite_note-137)

The higher educational institutions were predominantly German, but beginning in the 1870s, language shifts began to occur.[[128]](#cite_note-StraussPT196-138) These establishments, which in the middle of the 19th century were predominantly of German character, underwent in Galicia a conversion into Polish national institutions, in Bohemia and Moravia a separation into German and Czech ones. Thus Germans, Czechs and Poles were provided for. But the smaller nations also made their voices heard: the [Ruthenians](/wiki/Ruthenians "Ruthenians"), Slovenes and Italians. The Ruthenians demanded at first, in view of the predominantly Ruthenian character of rural [East Galicia](/wiki/East_Galicia "East Galicia"), a national partition of the Polish [University of Lwów](/wiki/University_of_Lw%C3%B3w "University of Lwów"). Since the Poles were at first unyielding, Ruthenian demonstrations and strikes of students arose, and the Ruthenians were no longer content with the reversion of a few separate professorial chairs, and with parallel courses of lectures. By a pact concluded on 28 January 1914 the Poles promised a Ruthenian university; but owing to the war the question lapsed. The Italians could hardly claim a university on grounds of population (in 1910 they numbered 783,000), but they claimed it all the more on grounds of their ancient culture. All parties were agreed that an Italian faculty of laws should be created; the difficulty lay in the choice of the place. The Italians demanded [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste"); but the government was afraid to let this Adriatic port become the centre of an irredenta; moreover the Southern Slavs of the city wished it kept free from an Italian university. Prime minister [Richard von Bienerth](/wiki/Count_Richard_von_Bienerth-Schmerling "Count Richard von Bienerth-Schmerling") in 1910 extended a compromise for it to be situated at once, provisionally in Vienna, and to be transferred within four years to Italian national territory. The German National Union (Nationalverband) agreed to the temporary hospitality for the Italian university in Vienna, but the Southern Slav Hochschule Club demanded a guarantee that a later transfer to the coast provinces should not be allowed, together with the simultaneous foundation of Slovene professorial chairs in Prague and Kraków, and preliminary steps towards the foundation of a Southern Slav university in [Laibach (Ljubljana)](/wiki/Ljubljana "Ljubljana"). But in spite of the constant renewal of negotiations for a compromise it was impossible to arrive at any agreement, until the outbreak of war left all the projects for a Ruthenian university at [Lwów](/wiki/Lw%C3%B3w "Lwów"), a Slovene one in Laibach, and a second Czech one in Moravia, unrealized.

### Universities in Transleithania

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Kingdom_of_Hungary_Literacy_1910.svg/250px-Kingdom_of_Hungary_Literacy_1910.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Kingdom_of_Hungary_Literacy_1910.svg)

Literacy in Hungary by counties in 1910 (excluding Croatia)

In the year 1276, the university of [Veszprém](/wiki/Veszpr%C3%A9m "Veszprém") was destroyed by the troops of [Peter I Csák](/wiki/Peter_I_Cs%C3%A1k "Peter I Csák") and it was never rebuilt. A university was established by [Louis I of Hungary](/wiki/Louis_I_of_Hungary "Louis I of Hungary") in [Pécs](/wiki/P%C3%A9cs "Pécs") in 1367. [Sigismund](/wiki/Sigismund,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Sigismund, Holy Roman Emperor") established a university at [Óbuda](/wiki/%C3%93buda "Óbuda") in 1395. Another, Universitas Istropolitana, was established 1465 in Pozsony ([Bratislava](/wiki/Bratislava "Bratislava") in Slovakia) by [Mattias Corvinus](/wiki/Mattias_Corvinus "Mattias Corvinus"). None of these medieval universities survived the Ottoman wars. Nagyszombat University was founded in 1635 and moved to Buda in 1777 where it is called [Eötvös Loránd University](/wiki/E%C3%B6tv%C3%B6s_Lor%C3%A1nd_University "Eötvös Loránd University"). The world's first institute of technology was founded in Selmecbánya, Kingdom of Hungary (since 1920 [Banská Štiavnica](/wiki/Bansk%C3%A1_%C5%A0tiavnica "Banská Štiavnica"), Slovakia) in 1735. Its legal successor is the [University of Miskolc](/wiki/University_of_Miskolc "University of Miskolc") in Hungary.[[129]](#cite_note-139) The [Budapest University of Technology and Economics](/wiki/Budapest_University_of_Technology_and_Economics "Budapest University of Technology and Economics") (BME) is considered the oldest institute of technology in the world with university rank and structure. Its legal predecessor the Institutum Geometrico-Hydrotechnicum was founded in 1782 by [Emperor Joseph II](/wiki/Joseph_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Joseph II, Holy Roman Emperor").[[130]](#cite_note-140)

The high schools included the universities, of which Hungary possessed five, all maintained by the state: at Budapest (founded in 1635), at Kolozsvár (founded in 1872), and at Zagreb (founded in 1874). Newer universities were established in Debrecen in 1912, and Pozsony university was reestablished after a half millennium in 1912. They had four faculties: theology, law, philosophy and medicine (the university at Zagreb was without a faculty of medicine). There were in addition ten high schools of law, called academies, which in 1900 were attended by 1,569 pupils. The Polytechnicum in Budapest, founded in 1844, which contained four faculties and was attended in 1900 by 1,772 pupils, was also considered a high school. There were in Hungary in 1900 forty-nine theological colleges, twenty-nine Catholic, five Greek Uniat, four Greek Orthodox, ten Protestant and one Jewish. Among special schools the principal mining schools were at Selmeczbánya, Nagyág and Felsőbánya; the principal agricultural colleges at Debreczen and Kolozsvár; and there was a school of forestry at Selmeczbánya, military colleges at Budapest, Kassa, Déva and Zagreb, and the [Imperial and Royal Naval Academy](/wiki/Imperial_and_Royal_Naval_Academy "Imperial and Royal Naval Academy") at Fiume. There were in addition a number of training institutes for teachers and a large number of schools of commerce, several art schools – for design, painting, sculpture, and music.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
