## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Economy_of_Austria-Hungary "Economy of Austria-Hungary")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/20-Kronen_banknote_%28Numista_source%29.jpg/250px-20-Kronen_banknote_%28Numista_source%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:20-Kronen_banknote_(Numista_source).jpg)

A 20-[crown](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_krone "Austro-Hungarian krone") banknote of the Dual Monarchy, using all official and recognized languages (the reverse side was Hungarian)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Schwarzer_Freitag_Wien_1873.jpg/250px-Schwarzer_Freitag_Wien_1873.jpg)](/wiki/File:Schwarzer_Freitag_Wien_1873.jpg)

Black Friday, 9 May 1873, Vienna Stock Exchange. The [Panic of 1873](/wiki/Panic_of_1873 "Panic of 1873") and [Long Depression](/wiki/Long_Depression "Long Depression") followed.

The heavily rural Austro-Hungarian economy slowly modernised after 1867. Railroads opened up once-remote areas, and cities grew. Many small firms promoted capitalist way of production. [Technological change](/wiki/Technological_change "Technological change") accelerated [industrialization](/wiki/Industrialization "Industrialization") and [urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization"). The first Austrian stock exchange (the [Wiener Börse](/wiki/Wiener_B%C3%B6rse "Wiener Börse")) was opened in 1771 in Vienna, the first stock exchange of the Kingdom of Hungary (the [Budapest Stock Exchange](/wiki/Budapest_Stock_Exchange "Budapest Stock Exchange")) was opened in Budapest in 1864. The [central bank](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank") (Bank of issue) was founded as Austrian National Bank in 1816. In 1878, it transformed into Austro-Hungarian National Bank with principal offices in both Vienna and Budapest.[[132]](#cite_note-142) The central bank was governed by alternating Austrian or Hungarian governors and vice-governors.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESugarHanak1990262-143) Austria-Hungary also became the world's third-largest manufacturer and exporter of electric home appliances, electric industrial appliances, and power generation apparatus for power plants, after the United States and the German Empire,[[134]](#cite_note-144) and it constructed Europe's second-largest railway network, after the German Empire. In 2000, a study estimated that GDP in constant national prices in 1913 was 19,140.8 million for Cisleithania and 10,971.6 million for Transleithania, a combined 30,112.4 million [krone](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_krone "Austro-Hungarian krone").[[135]](#cite_note-Schulze1-145)[[k]](#cite_note-146) According to a 2005 study, [GDP (PPP)](/wiki/GDP_(PPP) "GDP (PPP)") in 1913 was 105,515 million [1990 Int$](/wiki/Int$ "Int$"), the fifth-largest in Europe.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchulze200579-147)

The [gross national product](/wiki/Measures_of_national_income_and_output "Measures of national income and output") per capita grew roughly 1.76% per year from 1870 to 1913. That level of growth compared very favorably to that of other European nations such as Britain (1%), France (1.06%), and Germany (1.51%).[[137]](#cite_note-economic-148) However, in a comparison with Germany and Britain, the Austro-Hungarian economy as a whole still lagged considerably, as sustained modernization had begun much later. Like the German Empire, that of Austria–Hungary frequently employed liberal economic policies and practices. In 1873, the old Hungarian capital Buda and Óbuda (Ancient Buda) were officially merged with the third city, Pest, thus creating the new metropolis of Budapest. The dynamic Pest grew into Hungary's administrative, political, economic, trade and cultural hub. Many of the state institutions and the modern administrative system of Hungary were established during this period. Economic growth centered on Vienna and Budapest, the Austrian lands (areas of modern Austria), the Alpine region and the Bohemian lands. In the later years of the 19th century, rapid economic growth spread to the central [Hungarian plain](/wiki/Great_Hungarian_Plain "Great Hungarian Plain") and to the Carpathian lands. As a result, wide disparities of development existed within the empire. In general, the western areas became more developed than the eastern ones. The Kingdom of Hungary became the world's second-largest flour exporter after the United States.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchulze199680-149) The large Hungarian food exports were not limited to neighbouring Germany and Italy: Hungary became the most important foreign food supplier of the large cities and industrial centres of the United Kingdom.[[139]](#cite_note-150) Galicia, which has been described as the poorest province of Austro-Hungary, experienced near-constant [famines](/wiki/Famines_in_Austrian_Galicia "Famines in Austrian Galicia"), resulting in 50,000 deaths a year.[[140]](#cite_note-151) The Istro-Romanians of Istria were also poor, as [pastoralism](/wiki/Pastoralism "Pastoralism") lost strength and agriculture was not productive.[[116]](#cite_note-istro-125)

However, by the end of the 19th century, economic differences gradually began to even out as economic growth in the eastern parts of the monarchy consistently surpassed that in the western. The strong agriculture and [food industry](/wiki/Food_industry "Food industry") of the Kingdom of Hungary with the centre of [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest") became predominant within the empire and made up a large proportion of the export to the rest of Europe. Meanwhile, western areas, concentrated mainly around Prague and Vienna, excelled in various manufacturing industries. This [division of labour](/wiki/Division_of_labour "Division of labour") between the east and west, besides the existing [economic and monetary union](/wiki/Economic_and_monetary_union "Economic and monetary union"), led to an even more rapid economic growth throughout Austria–Hungary by the early 20th century. However, since the turn of the twentieth century, the Austrian half of the Monarchy could preserve its dominance within the empire in the sectors of the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), but Hungary had a better position in the modern industries of the [Second Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Second_Industrial_Revolution "Second Industrial Revolution"), in these modern sectors of the second industrial revolution (like machine building industry and electric industry) the Austrian competition could not become dominant.[[141]](#cite_note-152)

## Infrastructure

### Telecommunications

#### Telegraph

The first telegraph connection (Vienna—Brno—Prague) had started operation in 1847.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFichtner200969-153) In Hungarian territory the first telegraph stations were opened in [Pressburg](/wiki/Pressburg "Pressburg") (*Pozsony*, today's Bratislava) in December 1847 and in [Buda](/wiki/Buda "Buda") in 1848. The first telegraph connection between Vienna and Zagreb was contructed in 1850.[[143]](#cite_note-Brzojav_1850-154)

Austria subsequently joined a telegraph union with German states.[[144]](#cite_note-155) In the Kingdom of Hungary, 2,406 telegraph post offices operated in 1884.[[145]](#cite_note-156) By 1914 the number of telegraph offices reached 3,000 in post offices and further 2,400 were installed in the railway stations of the Kingdom of Hungary.[[146]](#cite_note-Kettős_kötődés-157)

#### Telephone

The first [telephone exchange](/wiki/Telephone_exchange "Telephone exchange") was opened in Zagreb (8 January 1881),[[147]](#cite_note-158)[[148]](#cite_note-Telephone_line_in_Zagreb-159)[[149]](#cite_note-160) the second was in Budapest (1 May 1881),[[150]](#cite_note-161) and the third was opened in Vienna (3 June 1881).[[151]](#cite_note-162) Initially telephony was available in the homes of individual subscribers, companies and offices. Public telephone stations appeared in the 1890s, and they quickly became widespread in post offices and railway stations. Austria–Hungary had 568 million telephone calls in 1913; only two Western European countries had more phone calls: the German Empire and the United Kingdom. The Austro-Hungarian Empire was followed by France with 396 million telephone calls and Italy with 230 million phone calls.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBroadberryO'Rourke2010[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidYHk0z-ujS3ACpgPA80_80]-163) In 1916, there were 366 million telephone calls in Cisleithania, among them 8.4 million long distant calls.[[153]](#cite_note-164) By 1914, more than 2000 settlements had telephone exchange in Kingdom of Hungary.[[146]](#cite_note-Kettős_kötődés-157)

#### Electronic audio broadcasting

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Telefon_Hirmondo_-_Stentor_reading_the_day%27s_news.jpg/250px-Telefon_Hirmondo_-_Stentor_reading_the_day%27s_news.jpg)](/wiki/File:Telefon_Hirmondo_-_Stentor_reading_the_day%27s_news.jpg)

A stentor reading the day's news in the Telefonhírmondó of Budapest

The *[Telefon Hírmondó](/wiki/Telefon_H%C3%ADrmond%C3%B3 "Telefon Hírmondó")* (Telephone Herald) news and entertainment service was introduced in Budapest in 1893. Two decades before the introduction of radio broadcasting, people could listen to political, economic and sports news, cabaret, music and opera in Budapest daily. It operated over a special type of telephone exchange system.

### Rail transport

Main articles: [Imperial Austrian State Railways](/wiki/Imperial_Austrian_State_Railways "Imperial Austrian State Railways") and [Hungarian State Railways](/wiki/Hungarian_State_Railways "Hungarian State Railways")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Austro-Hungarian_railway_map.jpg/330px-Austro-Hungarian_railway_map.jpg)](/wiki/File:Austro-Hungarian_railway_map.jpg)

Detailed railway map of Austrian and Hungarian railways from 1911

By 1913, the combined length of the railway tracks of the Austrian Empire and Kingdom of Hungary reached 43,280 kilometres (26,890 miles). In [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") only Germany had more extended railway network (63,378 km, 39,381 mi); the Austro-Hungarian Empire was followed by France (40,770 km, 25,330 mi), the United Kingdom (32,623 km, 20,271 mi), Italy (18,873 km, 11,727 mi) and Spain (15,088 km, 9,375 mi).[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBroadberryO'Rourke2010[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidYHk0z-ujS3ACpgPA80_80]-163)

#### Railways in Transleithania

The first Hungarian steam locomotive railway line was opened on 15 July 1846 between Pest and [Vác](/wiki/V%C3%A1c "Vác").[[154]](#cite_note-165) In 1890 most large Hungarian private railway companies were nationalized as a consequence of the poor management of private companies, except the strong Austrian-owned Kaschau-Oderberg Railway (KsOd) and the Austrian-Hungarian Southern Railway (SB/DV). They also joined the zone tariff system of the MÁV (Hungarian State Railways). By 1910, the total length of the rail networks of Hungarian Kingdom reached 22,869 kilometres (14,210 miles), the Hungarian network linked more than 1,490 settlements. Nearly half (52%) of the empire's railways were built in Hungary, thus the railroad density there became higher than that of Cisleithania. This has ranked Hungarian railways the 6th most dense in the world (ahead of Germany and France).[[155]](#cite_note-166)

Electrified [commuter railways](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail"): A set of four electric commuter rai lines were built in Budapest, the [BHÉV](/wiki/BH%C3%89V "BHÉV"): [Ráckeve](/wiki/R%C3%A1ckeve "Ráckeve") line (1887), [Szentendre](/wiki/Szentendre "Szentendre") line (1888), [Gödöllő](/wiki/G%C3%B6d%C3%B6ll%C5%91 "Gödöllő") line (1888), [Csepel](/wiki/Csepel "Csepel") line (1912)[[156]](#cite_note-167)

#### Tramway lines in the cities

[Horse-drawn tramways](/wiki/Horse-drawn_tramway "Horse-drawn tramway") appeared in the first half of the 19th century. Between the 1850s and 1880s many were built : Vienna (1865), Budapest (1866), Brno (1869), Trieste (1876). Steam trams appeared in the late 1860s. The electrification of tramways started in the late 1880s. The first electrified tramway in Austria–Hungary was built in Budapest in 1887.

Electric tramway lines in the Austrian Empire:

* Austria: Gmunden (1894); Linz, Vienna (1897); Graz (1898); Trieste (1900); Ljubljana (1901); [Innsbruck](/wiki/Innsbruck "Innsbruck") (1905); Unterlach, Ybbs an der Donau (1907); Salzburg (1909); Klagenfurt, Sankt Pölten (1911); Piran (1912)
* Austrian Littoral: [Pula](/wiki/Pula "Pula") (1904).
* Bohemia: Prague (1891); Teplice (1895); Liberec (1897); Ústí nad Labem, [Plzeň](/wiki/Plze%C5%88 "Plzeň"), Olomouc (1899); [Moravia](/wiki/Moravia "Moravia"), [Brno](/wiki/Brno "Brno"), Jablonec nad Nisou (1900); [Ostrava](/wiki/Ostrava "Ostrava") (1901); [Mariánské Lázně](/wiki/Mari%C3%A1nsk%C3%A9_L%C3%A1zn%C4%9B "Mariánské Lázně") (1902); Budějovice, [České Budějovice](/wiki/%C4%8Cesk%C3%A9_Bud%C4%9Bjovice "České Budějovice"), Jihlava (1909)
* Austrian Silesia: [Opava](/wiki/Opava "Opava") (Troppau) (1905), [Cieszyn](/wiki/Cieszyn "Cieszyn") (Cieszyn) (1911)
* Dalmatia: [Dubrovnik](/wiki/Dubrovnik "Dubrovnik") (1910)
* Galicia: [Lviv](/wiki/Trams_in_Lviv "Trams in Lviv") (1894), [Bielsko-Biała](/wiki/Bielsko-Bia%C5%82a "Bielsko-Biała") (1895); Kraków (1901); Tarnów, Cieszyn (1911)[[157]](#cite_note-168)[[158]](#cite_note-169)[[159]](#cite_note-170)

Electric tramway lines in the Kingdom of Hungary:

* Hungary: Budapest (1887); Pressburg/Pozsony/[Bratislava](/wiki/Bratislava "Bratislava") (1895); Szabadka/[Subotica](/wiki/Subotica "Subotica") (1897), [Szombathely](/wiki/Szombathely "Szombathely") (1897), [Miskolc](/wiki/Miskolc "Miskolc") (1897); Temesvár/[Timișoara](/wiki/Timi%C8%99oara "Timișoara") (1899); [Sopron](/wiki/Sopron "Sopron") (1900); Szatmárnémeti/[Satu Mare](/wiki/Satu_Mare "Satu Mare") (1900); [Nyíregyháza](/wiki/Ny%C3%ADregyh%C3%A1za "Nyíregyháza") (1905); Nagyszeben/[Sibiu](/wiki/Sibiu "Sibiu") (1905); Nagyvárad/[Oradea](/wiki/Oradea "Oradea") (1906); [Szeged](/wiki/Szeged "Szeged") (1908); [Debrecen](/wiki/Debrecen "Debrecen") (1911); Újvidék/[Novi Sad](/wiki/Novi_Sad "Novi Sad") (1911); Kassa/[Košice](/wiki/Ko%C5%A1ice "Košice") (1913); [Pécs](/wiki/P%C3%A9cs "Pécs") (1913)
* Croatia: [Fiume](/wiki/Fiume "Fiume") (1899); [Pola](/wiki/Pula "Pula") (1904); [Opatija](/wiki/Opatija "Opatija") – [Lovran](/wiki/Lovran "Lovran") (1908); [Zagreb](/wiki/Zagreb "Zagreb") (1910); [Dubrovnik](/wiki/Dubrovnik "Dubrovnik") (1910).[[160]](#cite_note-171)[[161]](#cite_note-172)[[162]](#cite_note-173)[[163]](#cite_note-174)

#### Underground

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/1896-17_vas%C3%BAt_%C3%A9p%C3%ADtkez%C3%A9s_Kl%C3%B6sz_Gy%C3%B6rgy.JPG/500px-1896-17_vas%C3%BAt_%C3%A9p%C3%ADtkez%C3%A9s_Kl%C3%B6sz_Gy%C3%B6rgy.JPG)](/wiki/File:1896-17_vas%C3%BAt_%C3%A9p%C3%ADtkez%C3%A9s_Kl%C3%B6sz_Gy%C3%B6rgy.JPG)

The start of construction of the underground in Budapest (1894–1896)

The [Budapest Metro](/wiki/Budapest_Metro "Budapest Metro") [Line 1](/wiki/Line_1_(Budapest_Metro) "Line 1 (Budapest Metro)") (originally the "Franz Joseph Underground Electric Railway Company") is the second oldest underground railway in the world[[164]](#cite_note-175) (the first being the London Underground's Metropolitan Line and the third being Glasgow), and the first on the European mainland. It was built from 1894 to 1896 and opened on 2 May 1896.[[165]](#cite_note-176) In 2002, it was listed as a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site").[[166]](#cite_note-177) The M1 line became an [IEEE](/wiki/IEEE "IEEE") Milestone due to the radically new innovations in its era: "Among the railway's innovative elements were bidirectional tram cars; electric lighting in the subway stations and tram cars; and an overhead wire structure instead of a third-rail system for power".[[167]](#cite_note-178)

### Inland waterways and river regulation

The first Danubian steamer company, [Donaudampfschiffahrtsgesellschaft](/wiki/Donaudampfschiffahrtsgesellschaft "Donaudampfschiffahrtsgesellschaft") (DDSG), was the world's largest inland shipping company until the collapse of Austria-Hungary.

In 1900 the engineer C. Wagenführer drew up plans to link the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube") and the [Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea") by a canal from Vienna to Trieste. It was born from the desire of Austria–Hungary to have a direct link to the Adriatic Sea[[168]](#cite_note-179) but was never constructed.

#### Lower Danube and the Iron Gates

In 1831 a plan had already been drafted to make the passage navigable, at the initiative of the Hungarian politician [István Széchenyi](/wiki/Istv%C3%A1n_Sz%C3%A9chenyi "István Széchenyi"). Finally [Gábor Baross](/wiki/G%C3%A1bor_Baross "Gábor Baross"), Hungary's "Iron Minister", succeeded in financing this project. The riverbed rocks and the associated rapids made the gorge valley an infamous passage for shipping. In German, the passage is still known as the Kataraktenstrecke, even though the cataracts are gone. Near the actual "[Iron Gates](/wiki/Iron_Gates "Iron Gates")" strait the Prigrada rock was the most important obstacle until 1896: the river widened considerably here and the water level was consequently low. Upstream, the Greben rock near the "Kazan" gorge was notorious.

#### Tisza River

The length of the [Tisza](/wiki/Tisza "Tisza") river in Hungary used to be 1,419 kilometres (882 miles). It flowed through the Great Hungarian Plain, which is one of the largest flat areas in central Europe. Since plains can cause a river to flow very slowly, the Tisza used to follow a path with many curves and turns, which led to many large floods in the area.

After several small-scale attempts, István Széchenyi organised the "regulation of the Tisza" (Hungarian: a Tisza szabályozása) which started on 27 August 1846, and substantially ended in 1880. The new length of the river in Hungary was 966 km (600 mi) (1,358 km (844 mi) total), with 589 km (366 mi) of "dead channels" and 136 km (85 mi) of new riverbed. The resultant length of the flood-protected river comprises 2,940 km (1,830 mi) (out of 4,220 km (2,620 mi) of all Hungarian protected rivers).

### Shipping and ports

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/KaiserFranzJoseph_Schiff.jpg/500px-KaiserFranzJoseph_Schiff.jpg)](/wiki/File:KaiserFranzJoseph_Schiff.jpg)

The [SS *Kaiser Franz Joseph I*](/wiki/SS_Kaiser_Franz_Joseph_I "SS Kaiser Franz Joseph I") (12,567 [GRT](/wiki/Gross_register_tonnage "Gross register tonnage")) of the Austro-Americana company was the largest passenger ship ever built in Austria. Because of its control over the coast of much of the Balkans, Austria–Hungary had access to several seaports.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Ragusa%2C_the_Inner_Gate%2C_Dalmatia%2C_Austro-Hungary-LCCN2002710786.jpg/250px-Ragusa%2C_the_Inner_Gate%2C_Dalmatia%2C_Austro-Hungary-LCCN2002710786.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ragusa,_the_Inner_Gate,_Dalmatia,_Austro-Hungary-LCCN2002710786.jpg)

[Dubrovnik](/wiki/Dubrovnik "Dubrovnik"), [Kingdom of Dalmatia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Dalmatia "Kingdom of Dalmatia")

The most important seaport was Trieste (today part of Italy), where the Austrian merchant marine was based. Two major shipping companies (Austrian Lloyd and Austro-Americana) and several shipyards were located there. From 1815 to 1866, Venice had been part of the Habsburg empire. The loss of Venice prompted the development of the Austrian merchant marine. By 1913, the commercial marine of Austria, comprised 16,764 vessels with a [tonnage](/wiki/Tonnage "Tonnage") of 471,252, and crews numbering 45,567. Of the total in 1913, 394 were steamers (tonnage 422,368), and 16,370 were sailing vessels (tonnage 48,884)[[169]](#cite_note-180) The Austrian Lloyd was one of the biggest ocean shipping companies of the time. Prior to the beginning of World War I, the company owned 65 middle-sized and large steamers. The Austro-Americana owned one third of this number, including the biggest Austrian passenger ship, the [SS *Kaiser Franz Joseph I*](/wiki/SS_Kaiser_Franz_Joseph_I "SS Kaiser Franz Joseph I"). In comparison to the Austrian Lloyd, the Austro-American concentrated on destinations in North and South America.[[170]](#cite_note-dradio-181)[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwiggum2008-182)[[172]](#cite_note-dame-183)[[173]](#cite_note-aeiou-184)[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWörthersee_Schifffahrt-185)[[175]](#cite_note-danube-186) The Austro-Hungarian Navy became much more significant than previously, as industrialization provided sufficient revenues to develop it. Pola (Pula, today part of Croatia) was especially significant for the navy.

The most important seaport for the Hungarian part of the monarchy was Fiume ([Rijeka](/wiki/Rijeka "Rijeka"), today part of Croatia), where the Hungarian shipping companies, such as the Adria, operated. The commercial marine of the Kingdom of Hungary in 1913 comprised 545 vessels of a tonnage of 144,433, and crews numbering 3,217. Of the total number of vessels, 134 were steamers (tonnage 142,539), and 411 were sailing vessels (tonnage 1,894).[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScott-Keltie1919[httpsarchiveorgdetailsstatesmansyearbo1919londuoftpage683_683]-187)

## Military

Main article: [Austro-Hungarian Armed Forces](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Armed_Forces "Austro-Hungarian Armed Forces")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/FRIEDBERG_Salomon_Emanuel_%E2%80%93_vojensk%C3%A1_par%C3%A1da_v_Praze_%281900%29.jpg/250px-FRIEDBERG_Salomon_Emanuel_%E2%80%93_vojensk%C3%A1_par%C3%A1da_v_Praze_%281900%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:FRIEDBERG_Salomon_Emanuel_%E2%80%93_vojensk%C3%A1_par%C3%A1da_v_Praze_(1900).jpg)

Military parade in [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"), [Kingdom of Bohemia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bohemia "Kingdom of Bohemia"), 1900

The [Austro-Hungarian Army](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Army "Austro-Hungarian Army") was under the command of [Archduke Albrecht, Duke of Teschen](/wiki/Archduke_Albrecht,_Duke_of_Teschen "Archduke Albrecht, Duke of Teschen") (1817–1895), an old-fashioned bureaucrat who opposed modernization.[[177]](#cite_note-188) The military system of the Austro-Hungarian monarchy was similar in both states, and rested since 1868 upon the principle of the universal and personal obligation of the citizen to bear arms. Its military force was composed of the [Common Army](/wiki/Common_Army "Common Army"); the special armies, namely the [Austrian Landwehr](/wiki/Imperial-Royal_Landwehr "Imperial-Royal Landwehr"), and the [Hungarian *Honvéd*](/wiki/Royal_Hungarian_Honv%C3%A9d "Royal Hungarian Honvéd"), which were separate national institutions, and the [Landsturm](/wiki/Landsturm "Landsturm") or levy-en masse. As stated above, the common army stood under the administration of the joint minister of war, while the special armies were under the administration of the respective ministries of national defence. The yearly contingent of recruits for the army was fixed by the military bills voted on by the Austrian and Hungarian parliaments and was generally determined on the basis of the population, according to the last census returns. It amounted in 1905 to 103,100 men, of which Austria furnished 59,211 men, and Hungary 43,889. Besides 10,000 men were annually allotted to the Austrian Landwehr, and 12,500 to the Hungarian Honved. The term of service was two years (three years in the cavalry) with the colours, seven or eight in the reserve and two in the Landwehr; in the case of men not drafted to the active army the same total period of service was spent in various special reserves.[[178]](#cite_note-Headlam_1911,_p._3-189)

The common minister of war was the head for the administration of all military affairs, except those of the Austrian Landwehr and of the Hungarian Honved, which were committed to the ministries for national defence of the two respective states. But the supreme command of the army was nominally vested in the monarch, who had the power to take all measures regarding the whole army. In practice, the emperor's nephew Archduke Albrecht was his chief military advisor and made the policy decisions.[[178]](#cite_note-Headlam_1911,_p._3-189)

The [Austro-Hungarian Navy](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Navy "Austro-Hungarian Navy") was mainly a coast defence force, and also included a flotilla of monitors for the Danube. It was administered by the naval department of the ministry of war.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeadlam1911b4-190)

## Successor states

Main articles: [Treaty of Trianon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Trianon "Treaty of Trianon") and [Treaty of Saint Germain](/wiki/Treaty_of_Saint_Germain "Treaty of Saint Germain")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Trianon_consequences.png/250px-Trianon_consequences.png)](/wiki/File:Trianon_consequences.png)

The [Treaty of Trianon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Trianon "Treaty of Trianon"): Kingdom of Hungary lost 72% of its land and 3.3 million people of Hungarian ethnicity.

There were two legal [successor states](/wiki/Succession_of_states "Succession of states") of the former Austro–Hungarian monarchy:[[180]](#cite_note-successorStates-191)

* [German Austria](/wiki/German_Austria "German Austria") (which became the [Republic of Austria](/wiki/First_Austrian_Republic "First Austrian Republic"))
* [Hungarian Democratic Republic](/wiki/Hungarian_Democratic_Republic "Hungarian Democratic Republic") (which after a few other short-lived intermediaries became the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary_(1920%E2%80%931946) "Kingdom of Hungary (1920–1946)"))

The 1919 Treaties of [Saint-Germain-en-Laye](/wiki/Treaty_of_Saint-Germain-en-Laye_(1919) "Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye (1919)") (between the victors of World War I and Austria) and [Trianon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Trianon "Treaty of Trianon") (between the victors and Hungary) regulated the new borders of Austria and Hungary, reducing them to small-sized and landlocked states. The Entente not only assumed without question that the minority peoples wished to leave Austria and Hungary, but allowed them to claim vast territories containing sizeable German- and Hungarian-speaking populations. With this in mind, in regard to areas without a decisive national majority, the Entente powers ruled in many cases in favour of the newly emancipated independent nation-states. The [Republic of Austria](/wiki/First_Austrian_Republic "First Austrian Republic") lost roughly 60% of the old Austrian Empire's territory. It also had to drop its plans for union with Germany, as it was not allowed to unite with Germany without League approval.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Hungary, however, was severely disrupted by the loss of 72% of its territory, 64% of its population and most of its natural resources. The Hungarian Democratic Republic was short-lived and was temporarily replaced by the communist [Hungarian Soviet Republic](/wiki/Hungarian_Soviet_Republic "Hungarian Soviet Republic"). Romanian troops ousted [Béla Kun](/wiki/B%C3%A9la_Kun "Béla Kun") and his communist government during the [Hungarian–Romanian War of 1919](/wiki/Hungarian%E2%80%93Romanian_War_of_1919 "Hungarian–Romanian War of 1919").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the summer of 1919, a Habsburg, [Archduke Joseph August](/wiki/Archduke_Joseph_August_of_Austria "Archduke Joseph August of Austria"), became regent, but was forced to stand down after only two weeks when it became apparent the Allies would not recognise him.[[181]](#cite_note-192) Finally, in March 1920, royal powers were entrusted to a [regent](/wiki/Regent "Regent"), [Miklós Horthy](/wiki/Mikl%C3%B3s_Horthy "Miklós Horthy"), who had been the last commanding [admiral](/wiki/Admiral "Admiral") of the Austro-Hungarian Navy and had helped organize the counter-revolutionary forces. It was this government that signed the Treaty of Trianon under protest on 4 June 1920 at the [Grand Trianon Palace](/wiki/Grand_Trianon "Grand Trianon") in [Versailles](/wiki/Palace_of_Versailles "Palace of Versailles"), France. The [restored Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary_(1920%E2%80%931946) "Kingdom of Hungary (1920–1946)") lost roughly 72% of the pre-war territory of the Kingdom of Hungary.[[182]](#cite_note-Columbia-193)[[183]](#cite_note-encyclopedia_of_ww1-194)

### Habsburg banishment

Austria had passed the "[Habsburg Law](/wiki/Habsburg_Law "Habsburg Law")", which both dethroned the Habsburgs and banished all Habsburgs from Austrian territory. While Karl was banned from ever returning to Austria again, other Habsburgs could return if they gave up all claims to the defunct throne. In March and again in October 1921, ill-prepared attempts by Karl to [regain the throne in Budapest](/wiki/Charles_I_of_Austria%27s_attempts_to_retake_the_throne_of_Hungary "Charles I of Austria's attempts to retake the throne of Hungary") collapsed. The initially wavering Horthy, after receiving threats of intervention from the Allied Powers and the [Little Entente](/wiki/Little_Entente "Little Entente"), refused his cooperation. Soon afterward, the Hungarian government nullified the Pragmatic Sanction, effectively dethroning the Habsburgs. Subsequently, the British took custody of Karl and removed him and his family to the Portuguese island of [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira"), where he died the following year.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The following states were formed, re-established or expanded at the dissolution of the former Austro–Hungarian monarchy:[[180]](#cite_note-successorStates-191)

* [German Austria](/wiki/German_Austria "German Austria") (which became the [Republic of Austria](/wiki/First_Austrian_Republic "First Austrian Republic"))
* [First Hungarian Republic](/wiki/First_Hungarian_Republic "First Hungarian Republic") which became the [Hungarian Soviet Republic](/wiki/Hungarian_Soviet_Republic "Hungarian Soviet Republic"), subsequently briefly restored and replaced by the [Hungarian Republic](/wiki/Hungarian_Republic_(1919%E2%80%931920) "Hungarian Republic (1919–1920)"), ultimately transformed into the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary_(1920%E2%80%931946) "Kingdom of Hungary (1920–1946)")
* [First Czechoslovak Republic](/wiki/First_Czechoslovak_Republic "First Czechoslovak Republic"), later "Czechoslovakia"
* [Second Polish Republic](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic"), contested by the short-lived [proto-states](/wiki/Proto-state "Proto-state") of [Tarnobrzeg Republic](/wiki/Tarnobrzeg_Republic "Tarnobrzeg Republic") and [Polish Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Polish_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Polish Soviet Socialist Republic")
* [State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs](/wiki/State_of_Slovenes,_Croats_and_Serbs "State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs") and the [Kingdom of Serbia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbia "Kingdom of Serbia"), both later absorbed into the [Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbs,_Croats_and_Slovenes "Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes")
* [Greater Romania](/wiki/Greater_Romania "Greater Romania")
* [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy")
* [Republic of China](/wiki/Beiyang_government "Beiyang government") (former [Austro-Hungarian concession of Tianjin](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_concession_of_Tianjin "Austro-Hungarian concession of Tianjin"))
* the short-lived Ruthenian (Ukrainian and Rusyn) [proto-states](/wiki/Proto-state "Proto-state") of [West Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/West_Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "West Ukrainian People's Republic") (later absorbed into [Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "Ukrainian People's Republic")), [Hutsul Republic](/wiki/Hutsul_Republic "Hutsul Republic"), [Lemko Republic](/wiki/Lemko_Republic "Lemko Republic"), [Komancza Republic](/wiki/Komancza_Republic "Komancza Republic") and the [Galician Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Galician_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Galician Soviet Socialist Republic"); all were ultimately absorbed mostly into Poland, but also into Hungary, Czechoslovakia, Romania and Yugoslavia.

The [Principality of Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein"), which had formerly looked to Vienna for protection and whose ruling house held sizable real estate in Cisleithania, formed a customs and defense union with [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), and adopted the Swiss currency instead of the Austrian. In April 1919, [Vorarlberg](/wiki/Vorarlberg "Vorarlberg")—the westernmost province of Austria—voted by a large majority to join Switzerland; however, both the Swiss and the Allies disregarded this result.
